q: A multinational financial firm manages 240 AWS accounts with strict data residency requirements: EU customer data must remain in eu-west-1, Asia-Pacific in ap-southeast-1, and US data in us-east-1. They need centralized security governance while allowing business units self-service account creation. What architecture ensures data residency compliance?
options: Single AWS account with IAM-based regional restrictions and S3 bucket policies
AWS Organizations with region-restrictive SCPs per OU, Control Tower account factory with region guardrails, and automated Config conformance packs
Separate AWS Organizations per region with no cross-organization networking
AWS Config rules that detect non-compliant resources and trigger automatic remediation
explain: AWS Organizations with OUs structured by region allows Service Control Policies with aws:RequestedRegion deny conditions to preventively block data creation outside approved regions. Control Tower's Account Factory automates guardrail application to new accounts, and Config conformance packs provide detective compliance verification. A single account with IAM policies (option A) cannot prevent all resource creation paths and lacks the organizational-scale governance needed for 240 accounts.

q: An enterprise has 180 AWS accounts across 12 business units with hybrid identity via Active Directory. They need consistent IAM baseline policies, permission boundaries enforcing maximum privilege per role, and audit trails for all cross-account assume-role activity. Which combination is most effective?
options: IAM roles in each account with trust relationships to AD
SCPs defining max permissions per OU, permission boundaries at account level, resource-based policies for SSO principals only, organization CloudTrail
AWS SSO with identity center, SCPs for baseline, CloudTrail logging
explain: SCPs establish maximum permission boundaries at the OU level (preventive control), while IAM permission boundaries act as a ceiling for individual role permissions regardless of attached policies. Organization-level CloudTrail with log file validation provides immutable audit records for all cross-account assume-role events. IAM roles with AD trust relationships alone (option A) cannot enforce org-wide permission ceilings or centralized audit trails across 180 accounts.

q: Your organization uses AWS Organizations with 250+ accounts spanning 8 OUs. You need to enforce all RDS databases encrypt, all data backed up daily, and all S3 buckets have versioning, with exceptions for sandboxes and dev. What's the most maintainable approach?
options: SCPs denying unencrypted resources; Config rules with remediation
Control Tower guardrails for preventive controls, Config conformance packs with OU-based exceptions, auto-remediation
CloudFormation StackSets deploying Config rules with manual exemptions
Service Catalog templates pre-configured for encryption
explain: AWS Control Tower provides AWS-managed, version-controlled guardrails for preventive and detective controls, eliminating custom maintenance overhead. Config conformance packs deploy Config rules at organization scale with OU-based exception scoping for sandbox and dev OUs. SCPs alone (option A) require custom development and lack the conformance pack's exception-per-OU capability and automated remediation integration.

q: A 200-account AWS environment needs three security domains: Production (PCI-DSS), Protected Health (HIPAA), Development (no compliance). Domains must never allow cross-domain network traffic. Each domain needs isolated billing and compliance reporting. How should OUs be structured?
options: Three root OUs: Prod-OU, PHI-OU, Dev-OU; SCPs blocking cross-OU assume-role and VPC peering/TGW between OUs; separate billing per domain
Single OU with tags and Config rules
Mix of OU hierarchy and tags with SCPs
explain: Structuring OUs by compliance domain (Prod-PCI, PHI, Dev) enables SCPs to preventively block cross-OU assume-role and VPC peering or Transit Gateway attachments between domains. Separate AWS Organizations (option B) would create three distinct billing hierarchies, preventing consolidated billing and making centralized governance impossible across domains.

q: Your organization has 340 accounts requiring consolidated AWS Bills with chargeback to business units by cost center, project ID, and environment. You need real-time cost visibility and anomaly detection. What's the best architecture?
options: Organizations billing consolidation with detailed CUR to S3, QuickSight dashboards per BU, Compute Optimizer for rightsizing
Cost Allocation Tags via SCPs, Cost and Usage Reports to S3, Athena queries, Lambda anomaly detection
AWS Billing Conductor with custom groups, Cost Explorer, CloudWatch anomaly detector
SCPs enforce tagging, then use Cost Explorer
explain: AWS Organizations consolidated billing automatically aggregates invoices across 340 accounts while the detailed Cost and Usage Report provides hourly, resource-level cost data exported to S3. QuickSight dashboards enable per-BU self-service analytics, while CloudWatch Anomaly Detection alerts on spend spikes. Billing Conductor (option C) enables custom pricing models for resellers but does not provide the real-time anomaly detection or granular CUR-level analysis needed at this scale.

q: An enterprise is migrating 150 AWS accounts from three unrelated Organizations into a single Organization for unified governance. Legacy Organizations use different tagging, naming, and SCPs. What's the safest sequence?
options: Keep separate, link via AWS RAM
Use AWS Migration Accelerator to auto-consolidate
Bulk invite all accounts, then apply Control Tower
Create target OU structure, migrate one-per-BU with tag reconciliation, gradually apply read-only guardrails first, validate with Config packs
explain: Bulk migration of 150 accounts risks SCPs and guardrails clashing with legacy configurations, breaking production workloads. The phased approach—OU structure first, per-BU invitation with tag reconciliation, then applying detective-only guardrails before preventive—allows validation at each step. Applying Control Tower immediately after bulk invitation (option C) would apply preventive guardrails to accounts whose resources were not assessed for compliance, causing widespread breakage.

q: Your organization operates 200 accounts and needs to implement Control Tower with existing custom CloudFormation guardrails already deployed. Which approach minimizes disruption?
options: Audit for equivalence, decommission duplicates, import accounts, add CT guardrails for additional coverage
Keep CloudFormation, add CT guardrails in detective-only mode
Deploy fresh CT, override custom guardrails
explain: Auditing existing CloudFormation guardrails for equivalence before decommissioning ensures no governance gap occurs during Control Tower adoption. Deploying fresh Control Tower and overriding existing guardrails (option D) risks removing controls that have no Control Tower equivalent, creating compliance gaps that auditors would flag.

q: A healthcare organization with 120 AWS accounts needs HIPAA compliance. Each account processes different patient data. They need per-account audit logs, centralized analysis, tamper-evident logging, and compliance evidence. What's the best architecture?
options: CloudTrail per account to S3 with MFA delete
Organization CloudTrail with MFA delete, Object Lock, log file validation, HIPAA conformance pack, Config aggregator
explain: Organization CloudTrail with S3 Object Lock in compliance mode ensures logs are tamper-evident and cannot be deleted by any principal including root. Log file validation with SHA-256 hashing detects any modification. Per-account trails to S3 with MFA delete (option A) provides weaker immutability than Object Lock compliance mode and lacks centralized analysis capability of Config aggregator for evidence presentation.

q: Your organization has 95 accounts across 5 business units with different cost allocation needs and autonomy. CFO needs consolidated billing, departments need independent tracking. Production must have hard isolation from development. What's the optimal structure?
options: One OU per BU with nested prod/dev OUs; use cost tags within each; SCPs limit cross-OU networking
Single prod OU, single dev OU; use tags for BU allocation
Flat OU with all accounts, use tags
explain: Nested OUs (BU level with prod/dev sub-OUs) mirror organizational hierarchy while enabling different SCP stringency per environment tier. Cost allocation tags within each OU provide granular chargeback data by BU. A flat structure with all accounts (option D) prevents environment-level SCP differentiation and provides no organizational isolation between production and development workloads.

q: An organization is implementing Service Catalog for self-service provisioning across 50 accounts. Business units provision databases, VPCs, compute. You need to enforce tagging, control costs, and prevent non-approved services. What's the architecture?
options: Central portfolio with approved products, launch constraints with cost tag requirements, SCPs deny non-catalog services, CloudFormation custom resources validate business logic
No restrictions, rely on Config remediation
Portfolio per BU with IAM policies, launch constraints enforcing tags, SCPs blocking non-catalog
explain: A central portfolio with launch constraints binding a specific IAM role ensures all resource creation flows through approved Service Catalog products. SCPs prevent direct resource creation bypassing the catalog. CloudFormation custom resources in launch templates validate business logic (cost center validity, naming). Delegated portfolio administration per BU (option C) provides no central enforcement of tagging or service restrictions.

q: Your organization has 180 accounts with AWS SSO from Active Directory. You need to ensure all humans assume roles, services use temporary credentials, and cross-account access follows least privilege. How should you architect IAM?
options: SSO for humans with permission sets, IAM roles for services, external IDs for cross-account
SSO groups to permission sets, SCP block IAM users, resource policies for SSO principals only, cross-account roles require MFA
explain: SCPs blocking IAM user creation force all identities to use temporary credentials via SSO. Resource-based policies restricted to SSO principals eliminate long-term access keys. Cross-account roles requiring aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent conditions ensure MFA is technically enforced, not just policy. Option A lacks the resource-based policy restriction and MFA enforcement at the cross-account assume-role level.

q: An enterprise with 220 accounts needs to share a VPC Transit Gateway across 15 accounts via RAM. The TGW needs to support hybrid on-premises connectivity via Direct Connect. How should this be architected?
options: TGW in network account, share via RAM, traffic routes through TGW
TGW with Direct Connect attachment, share via RAM, each account VPC attaches to TGW with own subnets
TGW in each account with peering
explain: A Transit Gateway in the network account with a Direct Connect attachment serves as the centralized hub. AWS RAM shares the TGW to 15 spoke accounts, allowing each account's VPCs to attach and gain transitive routing through the DX connection. Per-account TGW with peering (option D) creates N-squared peering complexity and does not support transitive routing for hybrid connectivity at scale.

q: Your organization manages 200+ accounts and needs to enforce compliance: all EC2 instances have detailed monitoring (1-min intervals), all RDS have 30+ day backup retention, all S3 have centralized logging. Which approach is most scalable?
options: Control Tower guardrails preventive/detective, Config conformance pack with remediation, org S3 bucket for centralized logging with Config aggregator
Service Catalog templates with Config verification
Org-wide Config rules with SNS and Lambda
explain: Control Tower guardrails are AWS-managed and updated automatically when AWS releases new versions. Config conformance packs aggregate compliance rules at organizational scale. Auto-remediation via Systems Manager handles non-compliant resources without operator intervention. CloudFormation StackSets (option C) require manual maintenance of rule definitions and cannot automatically scale to new accounts enrolled in Control Tower.

q: An organization with 160 accounts enabling CloudTrail organization trail needs: immutable logs, 7-year retention, per-account cost attribution, and <1s forensic query latency. What's the architecture?
options: S3 object lock governance, lifecycle to Glacier, Athena queries
S3 MFA delete + versioning, log file validation, Intelligent-Tiering, CloudTrail Lake for forensics, S3 Storage Lens for cost attribution
S3 object lock compliance mode, Athena+Lakeformation
Per-account trails with replication to central
explain: S3 MFA delete with versioning prevents accidental or malicious deletion of log files. CloudTrail Lake provides SQL-based forensic queries with sub-second response, far faster than Athena on S3. S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves older logs to cheaper storage tiers, optimizing 7-year retention costs. S3 Object Lock governance mode (option A) can be overridden by users with appropriate permissions, making it insufficient for tamper-evident compliance logging.

q: Your organization has 3-tier OU hierarchy (Root > Division > Department > Team) with 250+ accounts. Different tiers need different governance. How should SCPs be structured?
options: SCPs at each level: root (baseline security), division (residency), department (cost controls), team (service restrictions); union inheritance
Single set at root affecting all
explain: Hierarchical SCPs apply union-of-denies: a request must pass all SCPs in the inheritance chain from root to the account. Root-level SCPs establish universal security baselines, division SCPs enforce regional data residency, department SCPs add cost controls, and team-level SCPs add service restrictions. A single SCP set at root (option C) cannot differentiate between divisions with different residency requirements or departments with different cost profiles.

q: An organization consolidating 5 separate AWS accounts into single Organization has existing CloudTrail trails in 2 accounts and custom Config rules across all. What's the safest consolidation?
options: Create org trail parallel to account trails, validate 2 weeks, disable account trails; migrate Config rules to packs gradually via aggregator
Immediate org trail, disable account trails
explain: Running the organization trail in parallel with existing account trails for two weeks enables validation that no events are missed before account trails are disabled. Gradually migrating Config rules to conformance packs via the aggregator ensures detective controls remain active throughout. Immediately disabling account trails before creating the org trail (option B) creates a compliance gap where API activity goes unlogged.

q: Your 140-account organization needs encryption for all data stores (RDS, DynamoDB, S3, EBS). BUs have different key needs: AWS-managed, customer-managed, or HSM. How should this be implemented?
options: Per-OU SCPs per BU strategy; per-account keys; CT guardrails; Config with remediation
Per-account customer-managed keys; central account for HSM; Config detecting non-compliance
Single KMS key in central account via RAM
explain: Per-OU SCPs allow different encryption strategies: dev OUs permit AWS-managed keys for simplicity, regulated OUs require customer-managed or HSM-backed keys. Per-account KMS provides cryptographic isolation. Control Tower guardrails enforce key type requirements, and Config auto-remediation fixes non-compliant resources. A single KMS key shared via RAM (option D) violates isolation requirements and creates a single point of failure for all 140 accounts.

q: Your organization manages 200+ accounts with Firewall Manager for org-wide firewall policies (WAF, security groups, network firewalls). Some accounts need stricter rules. How should policies be structured?
options: Policies per security domain (dev, prod, sensitive), applied via scope targeting OUs; exclusion list for exceptions; local policies extend (not override)
Single policy set org-wide with Config exemptions
explain: Firewall Manager policy scopes enable domain-specific rule sets (dev policies more permissive, prod more restrictive, sensitive data accounts maximally restrictive). Exclusion lists exempt specific accounts from org-wide policies. Local policies in member accounts are additive to Firewall Manager policies, maintaining a security floor. A single org-wide policy with Config exemptions (option D) cannot represent different security postures and lacks the granularity needed for heterogeneous compliance requirements.

q: Your organization operates 250 accounts across 3 continents with GDPR residency. Requirements: data stays in-region, keys stay in-region, compliance audits per-region, global consolidated billing. What's the architecture?
options: Single Orgs at global, EU/APAC/Americas as regional OUs, regional SCPs, regional keys, per-region Config aggregators
Accounts pinned to regions via SCP, global key
Separate Orgs per region with RAM federation
explain: A single AWS Organization with regional OUs and region-restrictive SCPs enforces data residency while maintaining a single consolidated billing relationship and central governance. Separate Organizations per region (option D) eliminates consolidated billing, prevents org-level SCPs from spanning regions, and fragments identity governance requiring separate SSO configurations per Organization.

q: An organization with 95 accounts implementing RBAC via AWS SSO has custom permission sets per account, but 30% of permissions are identical. How should this be optimized?
options: Union all permissions in each set
Use IAM Identity Center policy inheritance
Create shared baseline permission set for all accounts, account-specific permission sets stack on top
explain: IAM Identity Center allows multiple permission sets to be assigned to the same user in a single account, enabling a shared baseline permission set plus account-specific permission sets without duplication. Creating individual permission sets per account (option C) requires maintaining 30% duplicated configurations across all accounts, increasing drift risk as policies evolve.

q: A 180-account organization implementing Service Catalog for provisioning must prevent shadow IT. You must block direct resource creation while allowing catalog use. What's the best approach?
options: SCPs block IAM user creation; launch constraints bind specific role; resource policies allow only that role; Config detects non-catalog with remediation
SCPs deny all creation except catalog role
explain: SCPs blocking IAM user creation eliminate the primary shadow IT vector (long-term access keys). Launch constraints bind resource creation to a specific Service Catalog role, making direct API calls fail. Resource policies allow only that role, and Config remediation terminates non-catalog resources. Option C (deny all creation except catalog role) would work but lacks the resource policy enforcement layer and Config detection for edge cases.

q: Your organization manages 300+ accounts enforcing complex compliance for sensitive data (PII, PHI, payment cards). Requirements: encryption, logging, exfiltration monitoring. What's the best architecture?
options: Macie for discovery/classification, org CloudTrail, immutable S3, GuardDuty for threat detection, SIEM via EventBridge
Encrypted S3, access logging, CloudWatch alarms
DLP via StackSets, per-account encryption SCPs, manual aggregation
S3 Block Public Access, per-account CloudTrail, local Config, manual reviews
explain: Amazon Macie discovers and classifies sensitive data (PII, PHI, payment card) using ML. GuardDuty detects behavioral anomalies indicating data exfiltration (unusual download volumes, access from tor exits). EventBridge routes findings to a SIEM for correlation and response orchestration. Using only encrypted S3 with access logging (option B) provides no active detection of exfiltration attempts or data classification capability.

q: An organization with 200 accounts needs to comply with requirement: no AWS secrets in code or environment variables. What's the best enforced architecture?
options: SCPs block IAM users, Secrets Manager for all secrets, resource policies restrict to service roles, CodeBuild pulls secrets at build time, CloudTrail logs access
Code review policies and SSO access
Parameter Store for config, Secrets Manager for secrets
explain: SCPs blocking IAM users prevent long-term credential creation. Secrets Manager stores all application secrets with automatic rotation. Resource-based policies ensure only specific service roles can access secrets. CodeBuild retrieves secrets at build time using IAM roles, so secrets never appear in environment variables or source code. Code review policies (option B) are detective, not preventive, and cannot enforce technical controls against secret misuse.

q: Your 120-account organization implementing auto-remediation for compliance violations. An S3 bucket created without encryption. What's the safest automated remediation approach?
options: Quarantine bucket with SCPs, manual remediation required
Lambda enables immediately, CloudTrail audit, manual review after
Config detects, Lambda enables encryption, SNS notifies team for approval, implements after confirmation
explain: Immediate auto-remediation risks breaking legitimate use cases (e.g., a developer creating an unencrypted bucket for testing before adding encryption). The Config detect → Lambda enable → SNS notify → manual approval workflow provides automation speed with human verification for potentially breaking changes. Immediate full automation (option B) may enable encryption on buckets where key permissions haven't been configured, causing application failures.

q: A 170-account organization needs to enforce MFA for all human access to production accounts. Teams have different MFA requirements (TOTP, hardware keys, Okta). How to implement?
options: SCPs deny assume-role without MFA, SSO with MFA, per-account IAM roles
Okta + SSO integration, permission sets with MFA conditions
IAM policies requiring MFA in prod accounts
Production OUs with SCPs denying assume-role without aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent, SSO with MFA, KMS-encrypted MFA seeds, CloudTrail verification
explain: Production OU SCPs with aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent deny conditions on AssumeRole prevent all console access without MFA. AWS IAM Identity Center enforces MFA at the identity provider level. KMS-encrypted MFA seeds protect hardware key authenticity. CloudTrail logs every AssumeRole event including MFA context. Option A lacks the resource-based policy restriction and doesn't specify KMS-encrypted seed protection required for hardware keys.

q: Your 250-account organization needs least-privilege networking with VPCs in 5 regions. All inter-account traffic routes through centralized hubs per region, all internet traffic through egress firewalls. What's the architecture?
options: TGW per OU with cascading peering
TGW per region with DX, cross-region TGW peering, centralized egress via network firewall
explain: AWS Organizations nested OU structure with TGW in the network account enables centralized DX termination and inter-account routing. Organization billing consolidation aggregates all accounts under a single master payer while TGW provides the networking layer. Single AWS account with IAM restrictions (option A) cannot provide the account-level isolation needed for 220 accounts and puts all workloads in a blast radius.

q: Your organization manages 200+ accounts auditing 500+ GB/day CloudTrail logs. Needs: <1s forensic query latency, per-account cost attribution, 7-year compliance retention. What architecture meets requirements?
options: S3 partitioned by account/date, Intelligent-Tiering, CloudTrail Lake for forensics, S3 Storage Lens for attribution
S3 + CloudFront, Glacier after 30 days, Athena analysis
CloudWatch Logs, Insights queries, 30-day default retention
explain: Organization-level CloudTrail with centralized S3 provides a single audit repository. Config conformance packs enforce compliance across all accounts. Detailed CloudWatch monitoring for EC2, centralized S3 access logs, and Config auto-remediation enforce the specific requirements. CloudFormation StackSets (option C) require maintenance per-requirement and cannot automatically scale to new accounts enrolled via Control Tower.

q: A 160-account organization implementing change management requires all infrastructure changes through approval process before deployment. What's the enforcement architecture?
options: All infrastructure via CloudFormation with peer review
Service Catalog with approval workflows, SCPs block direct creation, Config rules enforce
CodePipeline with approval stages, CodeDeploy to members
SCPs block CloudFormation except via approved system
explain: S3 MFA delete with versioning provides protection against deletion. CloudTrail Lake enables sub-second forensic SQL queries across the 500+ GB/day log corpus. S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically optimizes costs for the 7-year retention period by moving older logs to cheaper tiers. CloudWatch Logs (option D) has a 30-day default retention limit and does not provide the sub-second query capability or cost-optimized long-term storage needed.

q: Your organization has 180 accounts implementing chargeback with multi-dimensional allocation: business units, project IDs, teams, cost centers. Different services have different rules (compute per team, storage per BU). How to architect?
options: Enforce tags, CUR to S3, Athena + Redshift allocation, per-OU chargeback
Enforce 4 tags via SCPs, use Cost Allocation in billing, Cost Explorer grouping
Organizations + SCPs, detailed CUR to S3, Lambda cost engine with service-specific rules, cost reports to FinOps
explain: Service Catalog with approval workflows creates an auditable change process. SCPs prevent direct CloudFormation or AWS console resource creation, forcing all changes through Service Catalog. Config rules detect non-catalog resources for remediation. CodePipeline with approval stages (option C) does not prevent direct API-based resource creation and cannot block console-initiated changes.

q: A 200-account organization implementing Lake Formation for data lake serving 5 BUs with different access. Some data restricted to units, some shared. How to design?
options: Central account with RAM, databases per BU in S3, IAM-based access
Single catalog, database per BU, table permissions per unit
Central Lake Formation catalog, databases per BU, table-level permissions via cross-account roles, shared data accessible via permissions
explain: A custom Lambda cost allocation engine can apply different rules per service type (compute billed by team, storage billed by BU) using detailed CUR data—something AWS built-in tools cannot do. The detailed CUR provides raw cost data with resource-level granularity. Billing Conductor (option A) enables custom pricing models but applies uniform logic across all services rather than service-specific allocation rules.

q: Your 140-account organization runs hybrid with on-premises DCs via multiple DX connections. HA and failover needed across 3 AWS regions. How should this be architected?
options: Two DX (1 Gbps each) to primary for redundancy, VGW peering on-prem failover
Single DX to primary, Route 53 failover to secondaries
Single 10 Gbps DX with automatic routing
Redundant DX per region (min 2 each), different edge locations, BGP AS_PATH prepending, TGW per region with peering, on-prem BGP peers all DX
explain: Centralized AWS Lake Formation catalog with cross-account IAM roles enables BU-specific database isolation while allowing controlled cross-BU data sharing via Lake Formation permissions. Table-level and column-level permissions enforce fine-grained access. Separate Lake Formation per BU (option B) prevents cross-BU data sharing, creating data silos and requiring expensive data duplication for shared datasets.

q: An organization with 210 accounts implementing AppConfig for distributed configuration management. Different teams need different configs (feature flags, endpoints, timeouts). How should AppConfig be structured?
options: Applications per BU, environments per team/region, profiles per service, IAM roles restrict team access
Single application with environments per team
Centralized in management account, cross-account RAM access, applications per BU, IAM roles per team
explain: Redundant Direct Connect connections per region at different edge locations eliminate single points of failure. BGP AS_PATH prepending enables active-active or active-passive routing with automatic failover. TGW per region with inter-region peering provides the routing fabric. A single 10 Gbps DX (option C) has no redundancy—any DX physical failure or maintenance causes a full outage.

q: A 180-account healthcare organization implementing HIPAA audit logging needs: all API calls logged, logs immutable, compliance team only access for 6+ years, quarterly reports, encryption proof. What's the architecture?
options: Organization CloudTrail, S3 Object Lock (compliance mode), KMS encryption in compliance team's account, log file validation, quarterly reports via Config, access logging
CloudTrail to S3 with MFA delete, lifecycle to Glacier
explain: Centralizing AWS AppConfig in the management account with AWS RAM sharing enables cross-account access while maintaining a single source of truth. IAM roles per team enforce fine-grained access control to specific applications and profiles. Per-account AppConfig without sync (option D) causes configuration drift between accounts and lacks centralized audit capability.

q: Your 165-account organization implementing automated incident response: GuardDuty detects threat, system should isolate resource, preserve logs, alert team. What's the architecture?
options: GuardDuty → CloudWatch Events → Lambda isolates resource, SNS alerts
GuardDuty per account with local automation
Org GuardDuty with delegated admin, findings → EventBridge → Step Functions: Lambda isolates (revoke creds, remove SG), EBS snapshot for forensics, SNS alerts, Systems Manager collects logs
explain: S3 Object Lock in compliance mode provides WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage that cannot be overridden even by root. KMS keys owned by the compliance team's account ensure cryptographic control. Log file validation provides SHA-256 hash chains proving no tampering. Per-account CloudTrail with replication (option A) lacks the compliance-mode immutability and does not provide the cryptographic ownership proof required for HIPAA evidence.

q: A 195-account organization implementing cross-account backup/DR for production databases (RDS, DynamoDB) in 20 primary accounts to central DR account with automatic failover capability. How to architect?
options: RDS read replicas in DR, Global Tables for DynamoDB
AWS Backup with copy rules to central DR, cross-account IAM roles, automated DynamoDB on-demand backups with replication, pre-configured restore role in DR
Local RDS backups, manual copy to DR, manual RDS replica promotion
Backup agent per account, centralized backup server
explain: Organization-level GuardDuty with delegated admin enables centralized findings management. EventBridge routes findings to Step Functions, which orchestrate: credential revocation via IAM, security group removal, EBS snapshot for forensics, and Systems Manager log collection. This preserves evidence while containing the threat. A simple GuardDuty→CloudWatch→Lambda chain (option A) lacks the coordination for multi-step response and forensic preservation.

q: Your 200-account organization needs to enforce data classification and prevent exfiltration. All data classified (public, internal, confidential, restricted), attempts blocked/alerted. What's the architecture?
options: Macie for classification, S3 bucket policies block unclassified, Config detects
Macie tagging, IAM policies per classification
Macie for discovery/classification, SCPs block S3 GetObject on unclassified, GuardDuty detects bulk downloads, DLP Gateway blocks network exfil
Manual classification, Config enforcement, Lambda remediation
explain: AWS Backup with cross-account copy rules automates backup replication from 20 primary accounts to the central DR account. Pre-configured restore IAM roles in the DR account minimize RTO by eliminating permission configuration during a crisis. Local RDS backups with manual copy (option C) creates a multi-hour manual process that cannot meet the automated failover requirement.

q: A 220-account organization consolidating VPCs from multiple legacy environments into hub-and-spoke. 50 legacy VPCs across regions with different IP ranges and security group configs. What's the safest approach?
options: Bulk migrate all VPCs to TGW, test after cutover
VPC peering interim, plan TGW migration later
Create new VPCs, migrate apps via database tools
Create TGW infrastructure, validate 5 pilot VPCs for 2 weeks, waves of 20 VPCs/week with pre-flight validation (IP overlap, SG audit), post-migration testing, rollback per wave
explain: Amazon Macie automatically discovers and classifies data using ML, tagging S3 objects by sensitivity. SCPs can then deny GetObject operations on objects without approved classification tags. GuardDuty detects bulk download patterns indicative of exfiltration. Manual classification (option D) cannot scale across 300+ accounts and creates inconsistent classification that compliance auditors cannot rely on.

q: Your organization has 240 accounts implementing cross-account backup and recovery for Kubernetes (EKS) clusters with 20 primary clusters. What's the architecture?
options: AWS Backup for EKS with central account
Container image scanning, registry replication, assume stateless
EKS etcd snapshots in account, no cross-account
Velero deployed in each cluster, snapshots to central S3 via cross-account role, cross-account restore role pre-configured in DR
explain: Phased migration with pilot VPC validation proves the TGW architecture before committing all 50 VPCs. Pre-flight validation (IP overlap detection, security group audit) prevents production issues. Per-wave rollback plans contain blast radius to 20 VPCs maximum. Bulk migration of all 50 VPCs (option A) provides no rollback capability and IP conflicts or security issues discovered post-cutover affect all workloads simultaneously.

q: A 175-account organization implementing encryption key management needs: customer-managed KMS in each account for isolation, but centralized key rotation/audit from security account, and DR keys. How to architect?
options: Multi-region KMS per account with auto-rotation, security account read-only audit role (GetKeyPolicy, ListKeys), central CloudTrail
HSM-backed keys in central cluster via PrivateLink
Customer-managed KMS per account replicated to security for DR, assume key admin role for rotation
Customer-managed KMS per account, all trust security account root
explain: Velero is the Kubernetes-native backup tool that captures cluster state, PersistentVolumeClaims, namespaces, and ConfigMaps. Cross-account IAM roles enable Velero to write snapshots to a central S3 bucket. Pre-configured restore roles in the DR account enable rapid recovery. AWS Backup (option A) has limited EKS support and does not capture Kubernetes object state—only EBS volumes, missing cluster configuration and application state.

q: A 205-account organization implementing Trusted Advisor checks org-wide with centralized reporting and remediation recommendations. What's the architecture?
options: Organization Support plan with API refresh, findings → S3 → Athena queries, QuickSight dashboard per OU
explain: Multi-region KMS with automatic rotation provides key material redundancy for DR. Read-only audit roles (GetKeyPolicy, ListKeys) in the security account enable centralized monitoring without granting key administrative permissions to the security account. Assuming key admin roles for rotation (option C) grants the security account excessive permissions that violate least-privilege principles.

q: Your 180-account organization implementing Systems Manager Fleet Manager for managing 10,000+ EC2 instances across accounts and on-premises. What's the architecture?
options: Central Systems Manager as aggregator, cross-account IAM roles, central patching via SSM documents, hybrid activation for on-prem, Patch Manager compliance dashboard
Session Manager per account with local patching
Independent per account with manual sync
Automation per account with local workflows
explain: AWS Organizations Support plan enables automated Trusted Advisor API access across all accounts. Exporting findings to S3 enables Athena queries for trend analysis, and QuickSight provides self-service dashboards per OU. Per-account Trusted Advisor (option C) provides no aggregated org-level view and requires manual aggregation, which is impractical at 205 accounts.

q: Your 185-account organization adopting AWS Outposts for on-premises infrastructure. How to integrate into multi-account Organizations architecture?
options: Separate AWS account via Direct Connect
Outposts in Organizations with own OU, TGW on-prem connection, hybrid deployment via IaC, SSO extending to Outposts
explain: Central Systems Manager with cross-account IAM roles aggregates management across all 180 accounts. Patch Manager maintenance windows provide centralized compliance tracking. Systems Manager Hybrid Activations onboard on-premises servers with the same agent, providing unified management. Session Manager per account (option B) prevents the centralized compliance visibility and requires separate maintenance windows per account.

q: A 200-account financial organization implementing FinSpace for financial data management. Different BUs have different regulatory requirements. How to architect?
options: Separate FinSpace per BU for isolation
Single account, BUs access via API with tags
Single environment, data partitioned per BU via IAM
Dataset per BU with cross-BU sharing via FinSpace permissions, centralized ingestion via Kinesis/S3, BU workspaces with restricted access, cross-account IAM roles
explain: AWS Outposts placed within an Organizations OU inherits all SCPs, Control Tower guardrails, and Config rules, ensuring consistent governance. TGW on-premises connections extend hybrid networking through the same TGW fabric as cloud VPCs. Treating Outposts as a separate AWS account outside Organizations (option A) breaks governance continuity and prevents SCPs from applying to Outposts resources.

q: Your 195-account organization migrating from on-premises CA-signed certificates to AWS ACM. 5,000+ services depend on these certificates. How to manage migration?
options: Import per account for isolation with sync
Bulk import CA certs to ACM in primary account
Import CA key to ACM in security account, cross-account IAM roles, enable auto-renewal, migrate services in 200/week waves with validation, dual-cert setup during transition
explain: FinSpace provides a managed environment for financial data with built-in compliance controls, audit logging, and fine-grained dataset permissions. Per-BU datasets with cross-BU sharing via FinSpace permissions enforce regulatory boundaries. Separate FinSpace per BU (option A) prevents the controlled cross-BU data sharing required for analytics and creates data silos requiring expensive duplication.

q: A 205-account organization implementing continuous Config compliance monitoring. Configuration drift must be detected within 1 hour. What's the architecture?
options: Config per account with central aggregator
Continuous Config recording, per-account analysis, quarterly reviews
Org-wide Config via Control Tower with 1-hour recorder frequency, multi-region aggregator, conformance packs, non-compliant → EventBridge → SNS alerts and Systems Manager remediation
explain: Importing the CA key to ACM with cross-account IAM roles enables ACM to issue certificates as the organizational CA. Phased migration in 200-service waves with dual-cert setup prevents service outages—both old and new certificates are valid during transition. Bulk import of the CA cert to a single account (option C) doesn't enable ACM to issue new certificates from the CA and requires all 5,000 services to update simultaneously.

q: Your organization with 310 accounts needs sub-second cost attribution for 500+ services. Different business models apply per service (consumption, reserved capacity, hybrid). How to architect?
options: Billing Conductor with service-specific groups and custom pricing
S3 bucketing by cost center, Athena cost analysis
Detailed CUR partitioned by service, Redshift data warehouse with dimensional schema (service, BU, account, time), BI tools with row-level security
Cost Allocation Tags with Cost Explorer grouping
explain: Config continuous recording with one-hour frequency combined with conformance packs enables systematic drift detection across 205 accounts. EventBridge routes non-compliant findings to SNS for alerts and Systems Manager for automated remediation. Periodic snapshots (option C) introduce up to one-hour gaps in detection and do not enable automated remediation via EventBridge integration.

q: A 280-account organization implementing Grafana across hybrid (AWS + on-premises). Different teams need visibility into own resources only. Central team needs org-wide view. How to architect?
options: Central Grafana EC2 with SSH access per team
Per-team Grafana instances with data syncing
CloudWatch agent on all instances, export to Grafana via API
Grafana Cloud for managed service, AWS data source with cross-account IAM roles, on-prem agents send metrics, team folders with RBAC
explain: Amazon Redshift dimensional schema with Redshift Spectrum for S3 data access enables sub-second cost attribution queries with complex joins across service, BU, account, and time dimensions. Row-level security isolates BU cost views. Cost and Usage Report to S3 with Athena (option A) cannot support the complex multi-dimensional joins needed for 500+ services with hybrid business models at sub-second query latency.

q: Your 225-account organization implements automated account decommissioning: old accounts must be deleted, data archived 7 years, billing consolidated. Process involves 50+ account deletions monthly. How to automate?
options: AWS Batch jobs for data export to central S3, Config compliance check, close account via AWS Organizations API
Manual AWS account suspension, data export
Automation account with Systems Manager, scheduled daily: identify deletable accounts (via tag), backup via AWS Backup, clean resources per deletion SCP, close via org API
explain: Amazon Managed Grafana with cross-account IAM roles aggregates AWS CloudWatch data from all accounts. On-premises agents (Prometheus, Grafana Agent) ship metrics to Managed Grafana. Team RBAC via Grafana folders isolates team visibility. A centralized Grafana EC2 instance (option A) provides no managed service benefits and requires SSH access management for 280+ accounts, creating significant operational overhead.

q: A 190-account organization with 15 teams implementing Spot Fleet for HPC workloads. Different teams have different cost targets and capacity needs. How to manage?
options: Per-team Spot Fleet with capacity reservations per team, allocation strategy (lowest-price vs diversified), AWS Batch for job queueing, cost tracking per team via tags
Single Spot Fleet shared by all teams
explain: Systems Manager Automation with a scheduled daily trigger identifies accounts tagged for deletion, runs AWS Backup for archival, removes resources per a deletion SCP policy, and closes the account via the AWS Organizations API. This eliminates human error in the deletion order. Manual account suspension (option B) cannot scale to 50 accounts monthly and risks inconsistent deletion order that leaves orphaned resources.

q: Your 200-account organization implementing multi-region disaster recovery for databases. RPO must be <1 minute, RTO <15 minutes. 150 databases across 3 regions. How to architect?
options: Aurora Global Database for MySQL/PostgreSQL (RPO <1s), DMS continuous replication for others, Route 53 health check → Lambda promotes read replica, Systems Manager activation for data validation
Read replicas per region, Route 53 health checks, manual promotion
explain: Per-team Spot Fleets with individually configured capacity reservations ensure each team's HPC jobs are isolated from other teams' spot interruption rates. Diversified allocation strategy (multiple instance types and AZs) reduces the probability of simultaneous Spot reclamation. AWS Batch manages queued job orchestration. A single shared Spot Fleet (option C) creates resource contention and cost attribution complexity across 15 teams.

q: A 240-account organization implementing LakeFormation for PII data governance. PII must not be accessed outside 2 principal geographies. How to enforce?
options: Databases per geography, manual access review
Lake Formation table permissions via cross-account roles with geo-restriction tags, Macie + EventBridge block non-geo access attempts, log table access
S3 bucket policy block public access
explain: Aurora Global Database achieves RPO <1 second natively through log-based replication with <1-second replication lag. For non-Aurora databases, AWS DMS continuous replication with Change Data Capture maintains near-real-time sync. Lambda triggered by Route 53 health check failures promotes the read replica to primary, enabling RTO <15 minutes. Database snapshots every hour (option D) cannot meet RPO <1 minute and require full restore cycles making RTO >>15 minutes.

q: Your 170-account organization implements DMS (Database Migration Service) to migrate 80 Oracle on-premises databases to Aurora on AWS. Minimal downtime (<5 mins), 24/7 operation, current production load 10 Gbps. What's the architecture?
options: Single DMS replication instance, serial migration
AWS DataSync with custom Oracle export

Dedicated DMS task per database, parallel instances, continuous replication weeks prior, LOB chunking for large migrations, CDC during final cutover with brief app pause, validation per database before committing
explain: AWS Lake Formation table-level permissions via cross-account roles tagged with geographic attributes restrict PII access to approved geographic principals. Amazon Macie continuously monitors S3 access patterns and triggers EventBridge alerts when PII is accessed from non-permitted geographic IP ranges. IAM policies with IP restrictions (option C) are static and cannot account for VPN usage or corporate proxy egress, which would bypass IP-based geo-restrictions.

q: A 185-account healthcare organization implementing SFTP gateway on AWS for on-premises partner data exchanges. HIPAA requires encryption in transit and at rest, audit trails, per-partner access control, weekly rotation of credentials. How to architect?
options: AWS Transfer Family (SFTP) in VPC with private ELB, LDAP auth to on-premises AD, S3 backend with encryption+versioning, CloudTrail logging all transfers, automated credential rotation via Secrets Manager + Lambda
EC2 running OpenSSH with user accounts
explain: Parallel DMS tasks (one per database) leverage continuous CDC replication running for weeks before cutover, minimizing the final cutover window to minutes. Cutover strategy varies by tolerance: zero-downtime uses Aurora read replica promotion, 1-hour uses scheduled reboot with DMS cutover, 1-day uses batch export. A single DMS replication instance with serial migration (option A) cannot handle 80 databases concurrently and creates a multi-week single-threaded bottleneck.

q: Your 210-account organization managing 50,000+ EC2 instances implements automated patching across all accounts and regions. Windows & Linux, monthly patches, 99.95% uptime requirement during patching window. How to architect?
options: EC2 ImageBuilder for automated AMI patching
Run Command per account, manual batching

Systems Manager Patch Manager with cross-account role, maintenance windows per region (staggered), auto-reboot with health check groups (ASG, NLB target deregistration), rollback groups if errors detected
explain: AWS Transfer Family provides managed SFTP with VPC endpoint deployment for private networking, eliminating public SFTP exposure. LDAP authentication integrates with existing on-premises AD without requiring password migration. S3 backend with SSE-KMS provides HIPAA-grade encryption. Automated credential rotation via Secrets Manager + Lambda satisfies the weekly rotation requirement. EC2 OpenSSH (option D) requires manual credential management and provides no managed compliance controls.

q: A 220-account organization implementing AppStream 2.0 for 5,000+ distributed workforce to access on-premises applications without VPN. Low-latency is critical. How to architect?
options: Single AppStream 2.0 fleet in primary region

Regional fleets in 4 major regions with connection preference based on latency, hybrid gateway for on-premises app routing via Direct Connect, cross-region user/fleet profile replication, load balancing via Route 53 latency policy
Desktop AMI shared via Systems Manager Session Manager
explain: Systems Manager Patch Manager with staggered regional maintenance windows and health-check-aware target deregistration (removing instances from NLB target groups before rebooting) maintains 99.95% uptime. Auto-reboot with ASG health checks ensures failed patches trigger rollback to previous AMI. Manual per-account patching (option A) cannot coordinate 50,000 instances simultaneously and cannot maintain 99.95% uptime due to uncoordinated reboots.

q: Your 200-account organization implements EventBridge to orchestrate 500+ microservices across AWS and on-premises. Services have variable latency (10ms to 5s). How to ensure reliable event delivery and prevent cascading failures?
options: EventBridge with Dead-Letter Queues per service, exponential backoff, Rule-based routing with retry policies (max 2 hours), Circuit breaker patterns via Lambda, monitoring via X-Ray tracing
SNS for pub/sub, per-service retry logic
explain: Regional AppStream 2.0 fleets with Route 53 latency-based routing minimize user latency by connecting users to the nearest fleet. Direct Connect hybrid gateway provides the private network path from AppStream to on-premises applications without internet exposure. User profile replication across regions ensures session settings persist. A single regional fleet (option A) introduces network latency for users in other geographies, violating the low-latency requirement.

q: A 195-account organization implements KMS key management with cross-account key sharing for encryption. 500+ keys across accounts. Key audit and rotation must be centralized, but deletion requires approval from account owner. How to architect?
options: Keys in each account, S3 replication for backup

Keys per account, central CloudTrail for audit, cross-account assume role for rotation, per-account approval for deletion via Lambda approval gate, KMS key policy allows both account and central key admin
explain: EventBridge DLQs capture failed delivery attempts, and retry policies with exponential backoff retry over 2 hours prevent data loss. Rule-based routing enables per-service retry policies tailored to the 10ms-5s latency variance. AWS X-Ray traces event flows across hybrid infrastructure for failure diagnosis. Direct EventBridge without DLQs (option B) loses events when Lambda or downstream services are unavailable, failing the reliability requirement.

q: Your 230-account organization implements Secrets Manager with 10,000+ secrets. Audit team must see secret names and rotation status (never values), developers access only their app's secrets, emergency access via approval gate. How to architect?
options: Parameter Store for non-sensitive, Secrets Manager for sensitive
Secrets Manager cross-account resource policy, resource-based policies by secret (read-only for audit, read-only-values for devs), approval role for emergency access, CloudTrail logs all access
Central account with secrets for all teams
Secrets per account, IAM policies per developer
explain: Per-account KMS keys provide cryptographic isolation—a compromise in one account cannot decrypt other accounts' data. Cross-account assume-role enables the central security team to perform key rotation operations. A Lambda approval gate (triggered by deletion API calls) requires account owner confirmation before deletion executes. Centralized key storage (option A) creates a single point of failure and violates isolation requirements for 195 accounts.

q: A 175-account organization implementing AWS Marketplace for software procurement. Different BUs need independent Marketplace subscriptions, but central finance wants consolidated billing and license tracking. How to architect?
options: Single central account subscription, distribute via API calls
Marketplace subscriptions per BU account, bulk billing

Marketplace subscriptions in member accounts with linked payment method to master account, subscription tracking tag + Config rule validation, cost allocation report per BU, quarterly renewal approval via Approval Group
explain: Secrets Manager cross-account resource policies enable the audit team to call DescribeSecret (name, rotation status, tags) without accessing GetSecretValue. Developer resource policies grant GetSecretValue for their application's secrets only. Emergency access via an approval-gated IAM role provides time-boxed access. CloudTrail logs every API call including secret access events with principal identity. A central account holding all secrets (option C) violates least privilege and creates a single high-value attack target.

q: Your 205-account organization running Kubernetes (EKS) needs multi-cluster orchestration for resilience. 15 clusters across 3 regions. Cross-cluster load balancing, automated failover, state replication. How to architect?
options: Single cluster across regions via EKS Anywhere

EKS on EC2 + Fargate, Consul service mesh for cross-cluster service discovery, Global Accelerator for traffic distribution, EBS snapshots + Velero for state replication across clusters, RTO <5min failover
Per-cluster Helm deployments with manual sync
explain: AWS Marketplace subscriptions in member accounts maintain BU independence. Linked payment to the management account enables consolidated billing. Config rules validate subscription status and expiration. Approval workflows enforce quarterly renewal reviews centrally. A single central subscription account (option A) creates dependency on one account, prevents BU-specific product configuration, and doesn't enable per-BU cost allocation.

q: A 190-account organization implementing Compliance Manager for audits (SOC 2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA). 200+ requirements, different accounts have different compliance scope. How to aggregate and report?
options: Config rules per requirement, per-account audit

Security Hub standard + Compliance Manager with scope filtering per control, Config aggregator for org-wide compliance score, automated reporting to audit team via QuickSight, exceptions via approval workflow
explain: Amazon EKS with Consul service mesh enables cross-cluster service discovery and traffic management across 15 clusters. AWS Global Accelerator provides anycast IP addresses with health checks for cross-cluster load balancing. Velero with EBS snapshots replicates application state across clusters, enabling RTO <5 minutes. A single EKS Anywhere cluster (option A) cannot span AWS regions and does not provide the geographic distribution needed for multi-region resilience.

q: Your 215-account organization implements cost anomaly detection. Anomalies must trigger <5 min alerts to team, auto-remediation for known patterns, escalation for novel patterns. How to architect?
options: Cost Explorer trending alerts, manual review

Cost Anomaly Detector rule per cost dimension (service, region, account), EventBridge rule routes alerts to Lambda (for auto-remediation of known patterns) and PagerDuty (for novel patterns), Cost Optimizer recommendations auto-applied if pattern recognized from prior incidents
Cost anomaly detector with SNS notifications
explain: AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from Config, GuardDuty, and Inspector against compliance frameworks (SOC 2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA). Config aggregator provides org-wide compliance scores per control. QuickSight dashboards automate reporting to audit teams. Exception approval workflows provide documented audit trails. Separate audit tools per account (option A) cannot aggregate org-wide compliance and require manual reconciliation across 190 accounts.

q: A 200-account organization implements Amazon MWAA (Managed Apache Airflow) for ETL/data pipeline orchestration across 8 BUs. Each BU needs isolated environments, but shared metadata database and monitoring. How to architect?
options: MWAA environment per BU in member accounts, cross-account RDS for shared metadata, cross-account S3 logs for central monitoring, CloudWatch log group with cross-account role, IAM cross-account permissions for operators
Single MWAA environment, DAGs per BU
Glue jobs per BU instead of Airflow
explain: AWS Cost Anomaly Detector supports per-monitor configurations per cost dimension (service, account, region). EventBridge routes findings to Lambda for pattern matching against known anomaly signatures: if matched, auto-remediate; if novel, route to PagerDuty. The intelligence layer prevents the 20% false-positive alert rate that generic threshold-based detection creates. Increasing thresholds to 50% (option A) would miss real anomalies and provides no improvement to signal quality.

q: Your 225-account organization implements automated resource tagging compliance. All resources must have 5 tags (CostCenter, Owner, Environment, Service, DataClass). Non-compliant resources must be auto-tagged within 24 hours or terminated. How to enforce?
options: IAM policies require tagging at user level

SCPs require tag keys at creation time (with deny for missing tags), Config non-compliance triggers Lambda that tags missing-tag resources (infers from resource properties, account tags, or defaults), 24h remediation window, Config compliance dashboard
Config rules with SNS alerts, manual tagging
explain: MWAA environments per BU provide isolation for scheduling, secret management, and DAG execution environments. Shared cross-account RDS (PostgreSQL) for Airflow metadata enables coordination queries. Cross-account S3 log access with CloudWatch enables central monitoring without centralizing all environments. A single MWAA environment with DAGs per BU (option B) creates resource contention and prevents BU-specific dependency isolation.

q: A 185-account organization implementing least-privilege database access. DBA team needs emergent access to prod databases without standing permissions. Auditor needs to see what accessed what. How to architect for PostgreSQL on RDS?
options: Master credentials shared with DBA team
RDS Proxy with IAM authentication, temp IAM database auth tokens, RDS proxy logs SQL queries, Systems Manager Session Manager for DBA console access with approval, CloudTrail logs assumed roles
Database user per team member, manual grant/revoke
VPN access to RDS endpoint, database user per engineer
explain: SCPs with tag key deny conditions (aws:RequestedRegion + specific tag keys required) prevent creation of resources without required tags at the IAM policy evaluation layer. Lambda with custom resource logic infers missing tags from resource properties (account tags, resource type defaults) and applies them within the 24-hour window. Config dashboard tracks compliance progress. Config rules alone with SNS alerts (option D) are detective-only and cannot prevent non-tagged resource creation.

q: Your 240-account organization manages 15,000+ Lambda functions across all accounts. Deployment must be automated, consistent across accounts, and auditable. Any function version change must log who, when, what. How to architect?
options: CodePipeline with cross-account deployment role, SAM/CloudFormation for IaC, CodeBuild tests per account, automated rollback on alarm, CloudTrail logs all deployments, per-version Lambda environment variable logs change requestor and timestamp
S3 deployment zip per function, per-account Lambda execution
explain: RDS Proxy with IAM database authentication generates temporary tokens with configurable TTL, eliminating standing database credentials. Systems Manager Session Manager with an approval workflow provides auditable, time-boxed console access without VPN or bastion hosts. RDS Proxy logs SQL queries for full audit trail. Sharing master credentials (option A) violates least privilege, creates audit trail gaps, and cannot be time-bound for emergency access.

q: A 195-account organization implements automated backup and restore testing for RDS databases across all accounts. 500+ databases. Restore testing must occur weekly without impacting production, generate test data report. How to architect?
options: AWS Backup per account, manual testing
CloudFormation snapshots with per-week Lambda execution

AWS Backup with cross-account backup vault, Systems Manager automation weekly: restore snapshot to test account → data validation Lambda → drop test DB, generates test report per database, SNS alerts on failures
explain: AWS CodePipeline with cross-account deployment roles orchestrates deployments across 240 accounts from a central pipeline. AWS SAM/CloudFormation defines Lambda function infrastructure as code. CodeBuild validates tests per account before promotion. CloudTrail captures every Lambda:UpdateFunctionCode call with principal identity. Lambda environment variables recording change requestor and timestamp provide function-level audit context. Manual console deployments (option A) cannot provide deployment consistency or audit trails across 15,000 functions.

q: Your organization needs to interconnect 12 AWS regions with consistent, low-latency multi-region routing. 5,000+ services across regions, dynamic scaling. What's the most scalable architecture?
options: Transit Gateway in each region with multi-region peering, dynamic routing protocol (BGP), shared services in secondary regions via Resource Access Manager
explain: AWS Backup with cross-account vault policies enables centralized backup management across 195 accounts. Systems Manager Automation orchestrates the weekly test workflow: restore snapshot to test account, invoke Lambda validation (row count, checksum comparison), delete test DB, generate pass/fail report. Lambda validation ensures data integrity. Manual quarterly testing (option C) provides no automated coverage and cannot scale to 500+ databases.

q: A financial services firm needs active-active multi-region deployment with RPO = 0 (zero data loss). Customers expect <100ms latency globally. What architecture supports this?
options: Batch replication every hour to secondaries

DynamoDB Global Tables for real-time sync, Aurora Global Database for SQL databases, Route 53 geolocation routing, application state in DynamoDB (strong consistency within region, eventual across regions)
S3 cross-region replication with Route 53 failover
explain: AWS Transit Gateway per region supports thousands of VPC attachments. TGW inter-region peering enables cross-region routing. AWS Network Firewall deployed in centralized egress VPCs provides stateful inspection for all internet-bound traffic. A VPC peering mesh (option A) cannot support more than a few hundred VPCs due to non-transitive routing and routing table limitations.

q: Your organization runs hybrid cloud: on-premises VMware 10,000+ VMs, needs quick migration to AWS. VMs must remain on-premises during pilot. How to architect?
options: AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) only after commitment
Convert VMs to AMI via EC2 ImageBuilder
VMware Cloud on AWS: consistent hypervisor on-prem and cloud, vCenter extends to AWS, VMotion to cloud for zero-downtime migration, HCX acceleration for 100s of VMs/day migration throughput
explain: CloudTrail Lake provides a managed event data store with SQL query capability and sub-second response times, ideal for forensic investigation of 500+ GB/day logs. S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves logs between access tiers based on access patterns, optimizing 7-year retention costs. S3 Storage Lens provides per-prefix (per-account) usage statistics for cost attribution. CloudWatch Logs (option D) has limited retention, slow query performance for large datasets, and no sub-second forensic capability.

q: Your organization has 50+ petabytes of data in on-premises data center needing migration to S3 data lake. Network bandwidth limited to 100 Mbps. Timeline: 6 months. What's the best approach?
options: AWS Snowball Edge 100TB devices with on-prem cluster, 60-day round-trip, parallel imports to S3, ~840 devices for 50PB transfers
S3 Transfer Acceleration with HTTP connections
explain: AWS Service Catalog provides built-in approval workflows before product provisioning. SCPs deny direct resource creation (ec2:RunInstances, rds:CreateDBInstance) except via the Service Catalog role. Config rules detect non-catalog resources and trigger remediation. CodePipeline with approval stages (option C) only covers pipeline-based deployments and cannot prevent direct API or console-based resource creation.

q: Your organization needs a data lake serving 50 concurrent data scientists with sub-second query latency on 100TB dataset. SQL + ML workloads. Low operational overhead. What's the architecture?
options: Lake Formation + Redshift Spectrum for SQL (parquet optimized format), SageMaker for ML access same Lake Formation catalog, DW nodes for sub-second latency on dimension tables, Athena for exploratory queries
explain: Lambda cost allocation engines can apply service-specific rules that neither Cost Explorer nor Billing Conductor support—for example, charging Network TGW costs based on attachment hours and data volume per account, while charging shared RDS costs based on measured connection time. The detailed CUR provides the raw resource-level data for these calculations. Billing Conductor (option A) applies uniform custom pricing models but cannot implement the per-service, usage-based allocation rules needed here.

q: Your organization operates API platform serving 100,000+ requests/second. APIs use different patterns: REST (70%), gRPC (20%), GraphQL (10%). Need unified API management, rate limiting per customer, monetization. How to architect?
options: Three separate API services per protocol

API Gateway for REST/GraphQL (JSON transformation), Network Load Balancer for gRPC services, API Gateway Usage Plans for rate limiting, CloudFormation templates for consistent deployment, backend services independently scaled
explain: Per-account KMS keys with AWS Organizations trust policies in key policies allow the security account's audit role read-only access (kms:DescribeKey, kms:ListKeys) for centralized monitoring. Multi-region KMS keys provide disaster recovery key material. The central CloudTrail captures all KMS API calls. Replicating keys to the security account for DR (option C) grants the security account excessive access and violates the principle of key ownership isolation.

q: Your organization requires complex API authentication: OAuth 2.0 for web clients, SAML for enterprise, mTLS for service-to-service, mutual TLS for partner integrations. Single API endpoint. How to architect?
options: CloudFront with Lambda@Edge for protocol switching, API Gateway with multiple authorizers (Lambda for OAuth, Cognito for SAML, request/response transform), backend mutual TLS via ALB listener rules, partner mTLS via ACM client certificates
API Gateway authorizer supporting all protocols
explain: AWS Organizations Business/Enterprise Support enables the Trusted Advisor API for programmatic refresh across all accounts. Automated export to S3 enables Athena queries for trend analysis and QuickSight dashboards for per-OU reporting. Individual Business-plan accounts (option A) provide no mechanism for centralized aggregation across 205 accounts—each account's Trusted Advisor data is isolated.

q: Your organization runs real-time event streaming: 1M events/second, 100+ consumer groups, 30-day retention, sub-second latency delivery. Consumers are AWS services and external partners via HTTPS. What's the architecture?
options: Kinesis Data Streams with on-demand pricing (auto-scales to 1M EPS), Kinesis Firehose to S3 for 30-day archive, Lambda consumers for AWS services, HTTP endpoint for partners with delivery streams → Lambda transformer → HTTPS endpoint
SNS topics with SQS queues per consumer
explain: Central Systems Manager with cross-account IAM roles provides a single control plane for 180,000+ servers across all accounts. Patch Manager applies patches through maintenance windows and reports compliance status centrally. Hybrid Activations register on-premises servers with the same agent, eliminating separate tooling. Per-account manual patching (option A) cannot provide centralized compliance visibility and requires coordination across 180 accounts for each patch cycle.

q: Your organization implements IoT solution: 10M connected devices sending sensor data, event-driven processing (anomalies trigger alerts <1s), device management, fleet updates. How to architect?
options: IoT Core with MQTT/HTTPS protocols, rules engine routes to services (Lambda for alerts, DynamoDB for state, S3 for archives), IoT Fleet Provisioning for scalable device onboarding, IoT Jobs for OTA firmware updates, SDK on devices, TLS cert-based auth
explain: Outposts integrated into AWS Organizations with a dedicated OU inherit all SCPs and Config rules, ensuring consistent governance regardless of physical location. TGW on-premises connections provide the network path between Outposts and cloud VPCs. AWS SSO manages access to both cloud and Outposts resources with the same permission sets. Treating Outposts as a separate AWS account outside Organizations (option A) breaks SCP enforcement and unified billing.

q: Your organization needs a content delivery network for global applications. Primary concern: latency (users within 10ms of edge), security (DDoS mitigation, bot detection), custom origin logic (weighted routing to origins). How to architect?
options: CloudFront with Shield Standard (DDoS), Web ACL for bot detection + WAF, origin group (primary + secondary with auto-failover), Lambda@Edge for custom origin routing logic based on user geo/time/header, caching optimization per content type
explain: Amazon FinSpace provides managed financial data management with built-in compliance controls, audit logging, and permission-based access to datasets. Per-BU datasets with FinSpace cross-BU permissions enable regulatory boundary enforcement while allowing controlled sharing. A single FinSpace environment with IAM data partitioning (option C) cannot enforce the fine-grained regulatory isolation needed between BUs with different compliance frameworks.

q: Your organization runs microservices (500+ services) with complex inter-service communication. Services have different SLAs (99.9%, 99.99%, 99.999%). Traffic routing must respect service SLAs. How to architect?
options: Direct service-to-service calls with client-side retry logic
API Gateway for all inter-service calls

AWS App Mesh with Envoy sidecars, Traffic policy per service pair (retry count, timeout, circuit breaker based on SLA), X-Ray tracing for observability, CloudWatch alarms per SLA target, auto-scaling groups per SLA tier
explain: Importing the private CA key to ACM Private CA in a security account enables ACM to issue certificates as the organizational CA. Phased migration in 200-service waves with dual-certificate deployment (both old and new cert trusted) prevents service outages during transition. Auto-renewal via ACM eliminates manual renewal risk. Buying new cloud licenses (option A) rejects the requirement for CA key portability and results in a disruptive full certificate replacement without the dual-cert transition period.

q: Your organization has petabyte-scale data warehouse. BI team runs complex aggregation queries (OLAP). Data scientists run machine learning on same data. Workloads have different resource needs and latency SLAs. How to architect?
options: Redshift cluster for OLAP queries with materialized views, SageMaker for ML (direct access to Redshift or S3 via Redshift Spectrum), separate compute queues for BI vs ML to prevent resource contention, RLS (Row-level Security) per user/team
Separate Redshift + separate EMR for ML
Redshift Spectrum to S3 for both workloads
Single Redshift cluster with mixed workloads
explain: One-hour Config recorder frequency combined with EventBridge rules that trigger on non-compliant findings enables drift detection within the one-hour target. Systems Manager Automation provides automated remediation with approval gates for breaking changes. Multi-region Config aggregator provides global compliance visibility. Periodic snapshots (option C) create one-hour gaps in detection and miss configuration changes that occur and are reversed within the snapshot interval.

q: Your organization implements serverless application: Lambda functions, DynamoDB for storage, API Gateway for APIs, Step Functions for orchestration. Functions are latency-sensitive (P99 < 100ms). How to optimize?
options: Lambda provisioned concurrency (eliminates cold start), VPC endpoint for DynamoDB (eliminates NAT latency), DynamoDB on-demand with DAX caching layer for sub-millisecond reads, Step Functions Express workflows for short-lived executions
Lambda in VPC with ENI allocation
explain: Amazon Redshift with a dimensional schema (service, BU, account, time dimensions) provides the complex join and aggregation capability needed for multi-dimensional cost analysis across 310 accounts and 500+ services. Row-level security in Redshift isolates BU cost views via QuickSight. Detailed CUR to Athena (option A) can handle simpler queries but cannot support the multi-dimensional joins with sub-second response at the scale needed for 500+ services.

q: Your organization runs batch data processing: 100TB input, 50 billion operations, distributed computation. Jobs run overnight with <4 hour budget. How to architect for cost-efficiency?
options: AWS Batch with Spot Fleet (80% cost savings), job definition with retry logic for Spot interruptions, dependency DAG via job dependencies, S3 input/output with multipart transfer optimization
Lambda with SQS for distributed processing
explain: Amazon Managed Grafana provides a fully managed, scalable service with native AWS data source integration. Cross-account IAM roles aggregate CloudWatch metrics from all 280 accounts. On-premises Prometheus agents ship metrics to Managed Grafana. Grafana team folders with RBAC provide fine-grained isolation while the admin account has org-wide visibility. Centralized Grafana on EC2 (option A) requires self-managing scaling, patching, and multi-account IAM configuration.

q: Your organization needs to process streaming data: 100,000 events/second, complex transformations, analytics, and long-term storage. Sub-5-second end-to-end latency. How to architect?
options: Kinesis Data Streams for ingestion with auto-scaling, Kinesis Data Analytics for real-time aggregations and anomaly detection, Kinesis Firehose to S3 for archive, Lambda for complex transformations, SNS for alerts
explain: Systems Manager Automation with a scheduled EventBridge trigger identifies accounts tagged for deletion, calls AWS Backup to archive resources, applies a deletion SCP, and closes accounts via the Organizations CloseAccount API. Automation documents enforce consistent deletion order (snapshots before database). CloudFormation deletion roles (option D) only handle CloudFormation-managed resources and cannot manage manual resources or close accounts.

q: Your organization runs distributed training for machine learning models across 1000+ GPUs. Models take 10 hours to train. Infrastructure spans multiple AWS regions and on-premises. How to architect?
options: Manual distributed training orchestration with AWS Batch

SageMaker distributed training with Horovod (inter-node communication), custom training containers, multi-region training with EFA (Elastic Fabric Adapter) for low-latency inter-node communication, on-premises training via AWS Outposts, spot instances for 70% cost savings
Single EC2 p4d instance with multi-GPU training
Single on-premises cluster with cloud burst
explain: Per-team Spot Fleets with Spot Capacity Reservations ensure each team's HPC allocation is protected. AWS Batch manages queue-level job distribution. Allocation strategy (diversified across instance types/AZs) reduces Spot interruption probability. Reserved Instances for all teams (option D) would provide capacity guarantees but at much higher cost than Spot, preventing the HPC cost optimization that Spot Fleet enables.

q: Your organization requires HIPAA-compliant data processing: PII data, encryption everywhere, audit trails, access control, 6-year retention. Data includes patient medical records, genetic data. How to architect securely?
options: S3 with server-side encryption, IAM policies

S3 with client-side encryption (asymmetric keys), S3 Object Lock for immutability, VPC endpoints to prevent internet access, CloudTrail logging all API calls, GuardDuty for intrusion detection, Macie for data classification, BareMetalInstance for HSM-based encryption key management, Config compliance rules for HIPAA standard
explain: Aurora Global Database achieves RPO <1 second natively via transaction log replication with <1-second lag. For non-Aurora databases, AWS DMS continuous CDC replication maintains near-real-time sync. Route 53 health check failover with Lambda-triggered replica promotion achieves RTO <15 minutes. Database snapshots every hour (option D) cannot achieve RPO <1 minute—hourly snapshots represent 60-minute potential data loss, exceeding the requirement by 60x.

q: Your organization implements fraud detection: transaction stream (1M/hour), real-time scoring via ML model, blocking fraudulent transactions <100ms. Models update hourly with new training data. How to architect?
options: Kinesis Data Streams ingests transactions, Lambda invokes SageMaker real-time endpoint (A/B testing with production + canary), Model registry stores models, hourly SageMaker training job updates endpoint, SNS alerts for high fraud scores, DynamoDB caches transaction history for feature engineering
explain: AWS Lake Formation table-level permissions with cross-account roles tagged by geographic identifiers restrict PII access to approved geographic principals. Amazon Macie continuously monitors access patterns and triggers EventBridge when PII is accessed from non-permitted geographic locations. IAM IP-based policies (option C) can be bypassed via VPNs and corporate proxies, making them insufficient for enforcing geographic PII access controls.
