q: Your organization runs a SaaS platform serving 10,000+ customers with multi-tenancy. Tenants have different resource needs, SLAs, data residency. One tenant's infrastructure failure should not impact others. How to architect?
options: RDS Aurora cluster per silo (high-touch tenants) or Shared database with row-level security (standard tenants), separate VPC per security domain (data residency), cross-account isolation for premium tenants, AWS Glue data catalog per tenant for analytics
Single shared database with tenant ID column
explain: Parallel DMS tasks per database enable 80 concurrent migrations. Weeks of CDC-based continuous replication before the maintenance window means the delta to replicate at cutover is minutes of WAL. LOB chunking handles large objects that would otherwise fail DMS. Validation per database before commit enables rollback of individual database migrations without affecting others. A single DMS instance with serial migration (option A) creates a 80-database sequential queue incompatible with the 6-month timeline.

q: Your organization implements a serverless data lake: data ingestion (S3), cataloging (Glue), queries (Athena), analytics (QuickSight), security (Lake Formation). 500 users with different access levels. How to architect?
options: Glue crawler → Athena queries → QuickSight dashboards
Manual data management with SQL scripts

S3 data partitioning by date/source, Glue Crawler populates Catalog, Lake Formation permissions control access per user/group/table/column, Athena for SQL queries, QuickSight connected directly to Athena/Redshift Spectrum with RLS, Apache Iceberg for ACID transactions
explain: AWS Transfer Family provides managed SFTP with VPC endpoint deployment, eliminating public internet exposure. On-premises AD LDAP authentication integrates without password migration. S3 backend with SSE-KMS encryption satisfies HIPAA data-at-rest requirements. S3 versioning provides audit history of all file versions. Secrets Manager + Lambda automates weekly credential rotation without manual intervention. EC2 OpenSSH (option D) requires manual credential management and has no managed compliance controls.

q: Your organization manages hybrid infrastructure: AWS + on-premises + edge locations. Need consistent container orchestration, service mesh, observability. 500+ microservices. How to architect?
options: EKS Anywhere for on-premises clusters, managed EKS for AWS, AWS Service Mesh (App Mesh with Envoy proxies), X-Ray for observability, Control Plane managed centrally via AWS APIs, cross-cluster communication via Transit Gateway
Kubernetes manually on-premises, ECS in AWS
explain: Systems Manager Patch Manager with coordinated maintenance windows and NLB target group deregistration ensures instances complete in-flight requests before patching. ASG health checks automatically replace instances that fail post-patch validation. Staggered windows across regions prevent simultaneous regional patching that would reduce global capacity. Per-account manual patching (option A) cannot coordinate 50,000 instances and cannot maintain 99.95% uptime during uncoordinated reboots.

q: Your organization requires real-time anomaly detection across all AWS resources. 500+ accounts, 10,000+ resources. Anomalies must trigger actions <5 seconds. How to architect?
options: GuardDuty for intrusion detection, Security Hub for security findings, Trusted Advisor for infrastructure anomalies, EventBridge aggregates findings from all accounts via delegated admin account, Lambda analyzes patterns via ML model, SNS alerts + Systems Manager auto-remediation, X-Ray traces anomaly causation
explain: Regional AppStream 2.0 fleets with Route 53 latency routing ensure users connect to the nearest fleet. Direct Connect hybrid gateway routes traffic to on-premises applications privately, meeting security requirements. Profile replication ensures user settings persist across regional failover. A single AppStream fleet (option A) introduces 100-200ms latency for international users, violating the latency-critical requirement.

q: Your organization runs a video streaming platform: 100 Tbps peak throughput, global audience, 4K + HDR content. Users expect adaptive bitrate, offline download, <500ms start-up latency. How to architect?
options: AWS Elemental MediaPackage for live encoding + packaging, CloudFront global delivery with Lambda@Edge for bitrate selection, S3 for VOD with Intelligent-Tiering, DynamoDB for user state (playback position, bookmarks), offline download via Glacier Deep Archive with on-demand restoration
explain: EventBridge DLQs capture events that cannot be delivered, while exponential backoff retry policies with 2-hour limits prevent indefinite retry storms. Rule-based routing enables per-service retry policies matching each service's variable latency profile. AWS X-Ray provides distributed tracing across hybrid AWS and on-premises infrastructure to diagnose failure causation. SNS for pub/sub (option D) adds another hop and does not provide the per-rule retry policy granularity needed.

q: Your organization implements complex event processing: 1M events/second, state machines, complex join operations across multiple event streams, 99.99% accuracy, exactly-once semantics. How to architect?
options: Kinesis Data Streams, Kinesis Data Analytics for complex CEP (pattern matching, tumbling/sliding windows), Step Functions for state machine orchestration, DynamoDB for exactly-once idempotency tracking, Firehose to S3 for audit trail, X-Ray for end-to-end tracing
explain: Per-account KMS keys provide cryptographic isolation. Cross-account assume-role enables centralized rotation without permanently delegating key admin access. A Lambda approval gate intercepts kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion API calls and requires account owner confirmation before allowing deletion. All keys trusting the security account root (option D) grants the security account full key administrative capability, enabling deletion without account owner consent.

q: Your organization manages edge computing: 500+ edge locations with local GPU processing, cloud training, global model serving. Latency SLA: <10ms at edge. How to architect?
options: Inference via Kinesis at cloud only

AWS IoT Greengrass for edge deployment, SageMaker Neo compilation for edge runtime optimization, local inference with <10ms latency, cloud SageMaker for retraining, fleet-wide model updates via IoT Jobs, S3 bucket for model versioning
explain: Secrets Manager cross-account resource-based policies enable the audit team to call DescribeSecret (metadata only) while developers can call GetSecretValue only for their application's secrets. An emergency access approval-gated role provides time-boxed access. CloudTrail logs every API call. Per-account secrets with per-developer IAM policies (option D) creates management overhead proportional to the number of developer-secret combinations and cannot scale to 10,000+ secrets.

q: Your organization implements a serverless web application: API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB + S3. Application growth expectation: 10x traffic in 6 months. How to architect for elastic scaling?
options: Lambda with provisioned concurrency (eliminates cold start), DynamoDB on-demand billing (auto-scales), CloudFront caching with TTL optimization, S3 Transfer Acceleration for global uploads, auto-scaling Lambda provisioned concurrency based on predicted load via CloudWatch anomaly detection
Fixed Lambda memory, provisioned DynamoDB capacity
explain: Marketplace subscriptions in member accounts with linked payment to the management account enable per-BU independence with consolidated billing. Config rules validate subscription status and expiration dates. Quarterly renewal approval via an Approval Group provides a controlled procurement workflow. A single central subscription account (option A) creates a bottleneck for all Marketplace software access and prevents BU-level customization of subscription configuration.

q: Your organization requires a search platform: 100TB index, 1M queries/second, sub-100ms search latency, faceted search, spelling correction. How to architect?
options: Amazon OpenSearch on dedicated hardware, UltraWarm storage for historical data, cross-cluster replication for geo-distribution, k-NN search plugin for ML-based recommendations, Query DSL with SpellChecker plugin, CloudFront in front for query result caching
explain: Amazon EKS with Consul service mesh provides cross-cluster service discovery and traffic routing. AWS Global Accelerator provides anycast routing with health-based failover across 15 clusters. Velero captures PersistentVolumeClaim state for application data replication. Pre-configured restore roles enable RTO <5 minutes. EKS Anywhere (option A) is designed for extending Kubernetes to on-premises, not for multi-region AWS deployments.

q: Your organization runs a multi-protocol API (REST, GraphQL, gRPC, MQTT). Clients have varying latency tolerances (10ms to 5s). Single endpoint. How to architect?
options: Network Load Balancer with TLS SNI routing, API Gateway for REST (transformed by Lambda@Edge), AppSync for GraphQL (with DataSource connectors), gRPC service via target group (ALB supports HTTP/2), MQTT via IoT Core, unified auth via Cognito + Lambda authorizer
explain: AWS Security Hub with Compliance Manager aggregates findings from 190 accounts against SOC 2, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA frameworks. Config aggregator provides org-wide compliance scores per control. QuickSight dashboards automate quarterly report generation. Exception approval workflows track control exceptions with documented rationale. Separate audit tools per account (option A) cannot provide cross-account compliance scoring and require manual reconciliation that cannot scale to 200+ requirements.

q: Your organization implements ML Ops pipeline: continuous training (daily), model validation, A/B testing in production, model registry, automated rollback. 100+ models in production. How to architect?
options: SageMaker Pipelines for orchestration (data validation, training, model evaluation), Model Registry for versioning, SageMaker Feature Store for feature management, Lambda for A/B testing experiment orchestration, CloudWatch alarms trigger automatic rollback if model metrics degrade, CodePipeline stages for approval gates
explain: AWS Cost Anomaly Detector with per-dimension monitors provides anomaly signals within minutes. EventBridge routes findings to Lambda: if the anomaly pattern matches a known signature (via lookup table), auto-remediate (terminate idle instances, apply reserved capacity); if novel, route to PagerDuty for human investigation. Cost Optimizer recommendations applied only for known patterns limits risk. Manual cost review (option D) cannot meet the <5 minute alert requirement.

q: Your organization implements autonomous systems: vehicles, drones, robots. Real-time processing at edge, cloud training, fleet management. 10,000+ autonomous units. How to architect?
options: AWS IoT Greengrass Core on vehicles for local inference, Fleet Provisioning for onboarding, AWS IoT Device Management for fleet control, cloud DynamoDB for global state (location, telemetry), SageMaker for model updates, Systems Manager for OTA deployment, S3 for sensor data archive
explain: MWAA environments per BU provide isolated DAG execution, dependency management, and Airflow configuration. Cross-account RDS (PostgreSQL) for Airflow metadata enables DAG state coordination. Cross-account S3 log export enables central monitoring without creating a shared environment. A single MWAA environment with all BU DAGs (option B) creates scheduling conflicts, dependency interference, and prevents BU-specific Airflow configuration.

q: Your organization operates 5,000+ servers on-premises (VM-based). Legacy business applications, some with 20-year-old code. 2-year migration window. How to prioritize and execute?
options: AWS Cloud Adoption Framework: assess (dependency mapping, 6Rs classification), prioritize (quick wins + business value), migrate waves (Rehost/Replatform 70%, Refactor 20%, Retire 10%), AWS MGN for rehost, AWS DMS for databases
Rehost all servers to EC2 immediately
explain: TGW per region with inter-region peering scales to 5,000 VPC attachments per TGW. BGP enables dynamic routing and automatic failover between regions. AWS RAM shares resources across accounts within the Organization. Site-to-Site VPN between regions (option B) is limited to 1.25 Gbps per tunnel, cannot serve 5,000+ services, and adds significant latency compared to TGW inter-region peering.

q: Your organization has monolithic Java application (10M lines of code, 200 engineers, 5-year release cycle). Needs to become microservices for faster innovation (2-week releases). How to migrate?
options: Strangler Fig pattern: API facade in front of monolith, new features as microservices, gradually replace monolith components, API Gateway routes traffic, old components fall away over 2 years, no big-bang rewrite
explain: DynamoDB Global Tables provide sub-second active-active replication with eventual consistency across regions, achieving RPO approaching zero. Aurora Global Database writes to a primary region and replicates with <1-second lag. Route 53 geolocation routing directs users to their nearest region. Batch replication every hour (option A) creates 60-minute RPO, violating the RPO=0 requirement.

q: Your organization migrates 500+ databases from on-premises Oracle (multi-terabyte) to AWS. Different apps have different downtime tolerances (0 min, 1 hr, 1 day). How to execute at scale?
options: Lift-and-shift via Oracle Cloud VM import
AWS DMS with full-load + CDC for all databases

AWS Schema Conversion Tool (auto-convert schema), DMS with continuous replication (weeks before cutover), database-specific cutover strategy (0-downtime via Aurora read replica + switchover, 1hr via reboot, 1day via batch), validation post-cutover, rollback plan per DB
explain: VMware Cloud on AWS maintains the VMware hypervisor, vCenter, and vSphere stack on dedicated AWS hardware. HCX provides WAN optimization and bulk migration capability. vMotion enables zero-downtime live migration of running VMs. AWS Application Migration Service (option B) requires agent installation on each VM and cannot migrate VMs while running, requiring maintenance windows.

q: Your organization has 10,000+ traditional IT management servers (configuration, patch, deploy, monitor). Cloud-native apps emerging. Need unified management across hybrid. How to architect?
options: Custom management tool across all servers
Keep separate legacy and cloud tooling

AWS Systems Manager for unified management: Patch Manager for patching (hybrid agents), Session Manager for console access, Automation documents for runbooks, Ops Center for incident management, CloudWatch agent for monitoring everywhere, IAM for access control
explain: AWS Snowball Edge 100TB appliances handle physical data transfer where network bandwidth (100 Mbps = 1.08 TB/day) cannot complete 50 PB migration in 6 months (which would require 46,000 days). Parallel Snowball Edge imports enable 50 PB in 6 months. S3 Transfer Acceleration (option B) is limited by the 100 Mbps network constraint and cannot transfer 50 PB in 6 months.

q: Your organization migrates Windows Server workloads (Active Directory, SQL Server, Exchange, SharePoint). 200+ servers, complex interdependencies. Minimizes business disruption. How to migrate?
options: Replatform to cloud-native (eliminate AD, use Cognito, eliminate SQL Server, use RDS/Aurora)

AWS MGN (Application Migration Service) for Windows servers, AWS DataSync for file migrations, AWS Database Migration Service for SQL Server, AWS Directory Service for AD, migration in waves per application stack, parallel prod validation before cutover
explain: AWS Lake Formation provides centralized metadata catalog with fine-grained column and table level permissions. Redshift Spectrum queries S3 parquet data. SageMaker integrates directly with the Lake Formation catalog. Redshift DW nodes provide sub-second latency for pre-aggregated dimension tables. Athena alone (option B) provides ad-hoc query capability but cannot achieve sub-second query latency for complex aggregation queries across 100TB.

q: Your organization has containerized microservices running on-premises (Docker, Kubernetes). Needs AWS deployment without managing Kubernetes. How to architect?
options: ECS Fargate for containers (no Kubernetes), Service discovery via CloudMap, ALB for load balancing, auto-scaling via target tracking, CloudWatch logs for observability, AWS App Mesh for service mesh if needed
explain: API Gateway handles REST and GraphQL (via AWS AppSync or HTTP integration) with JSON transformation. Network Load Balancer handles gRPC (binary Protobuf protocol) via HTTP/2 target groups. API Gateway Usage Plans provide rate limiting and API key management per customer for monetization. Three separate API services (option A) fragment management and prevent unified rate limiting and monetization policies.

q: Your organization runs SAP ERP system on-premises (300+ users, 2TB database, mission-critical 24/7). Migration must have <5 min RTO, zero data loss. How to migrate?
options: Keep on-premises, use AWS for disaster recovery only
SAP HANA on AWS with data import

AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) for compute + AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) continuous replication for SAP database, parallel environment testing, Route 53 health check triggers automatic DNS failover to AWS, rollback procedure pre-tested, cutover during maintenance window <5 min
explain: Lambda@Edge inspects request headers, TLS client certificates, and path patterns to route traffic to protocol-specific handlers. API Gateway supports multiple Lambda authorizers—OAuth tokens via Cognito, SAML assertions via Lambda. ALB listener rules route based on client certificate attributes for mTLS. IoT Core handles MQTT. Separate endpoints per auth type (option A) violates the single-endpoint requirement.

q: Your organization has Salesforce on-premises customization layer (1M lines of Apex code, custom integrations). Needs to run on Salesforce cloud. How to migrate?
options: Salesforce Functions (serverless compute on SFDC platform), AWS Lambda for complex logic (via Salesforce outbound events), Salesforce DataCloud for unified data, AWS integration via VPC endpoints, Apex code gradually refactored to Functions
explain: Kinesis Data Streams with on-demand capacity auto-scales to handle 1M events per second without provisioning. Kinesis Firehose delivers to S3 for 30-day retention archival. Lambda consumers handle AWS-native service integrations. HTTP endpoint delivery streams enable external partner consumption. SNS with SQS per consumer (option B) cannot support 100+ consumer groups at 1M events/second with 30-day retention.

q: Your organization operates data warehouse (Teradata, 100TB, 2,000+ reports). Migration to AWS required. Some reports run nightly, some real-time dashboards. How to migrate?
options: AWS Glue ETL scripts replace Teradata ETL, Redshift DW for OLAP (nightly reports), Amazon QuickSight for real-time dashboards (Athena + S3 for ad-hoc), S3 acts as data lake, graduated license cost from Teradata
explain: AWS IoT Core handles 10M device connections via MQTT with built-in message broker scaling. Rules Engine routes messages to Lambda (for sub-second alert processing), DynamoDB (for device state), and S3 (for archive). Fleet Provisioning automates device certificate onboarding. IoT Jobs delivers firmware updates atomically per device. Custom Kinesis + Lambda (option A) requires building device connection management, security, and fleet management from scratch.

q: Your organization operates mainframe legacy billing system (CICS, 40 years old). Needs cloud migration while supporting legacy interfaces (batch files, async messages, FTP). How to architect?
options: Run mainframe in AWS via migration license

AWS Mainframe Modernization: use Automated migration tool to convert COBOL to Java, containerize into ECS, maintain legacy interfaces via API Gateway + Lambda transformers, API Gateway + ALB for FTP protocol support via custom handlers
explain: Amazon CloudFront with Origin Shield reduces origin load by providing an additional cache layer. AWS WAF with Bot Control detects and blocks automated traffic. Shield Standard provides DDoS mitigation. Lambda@Edge executes custom routing logic at edge locations before requests reach the origin. CloudFront Origin Groups provide automatic failover between primary and secondary origins. Regional ALBs (option D) require DNS-based routing with significant latency overhead and provide no edge caching.

q: Your organization has legacy LAMP stack websites (PHP, MySQL, Apache). 50+ websites, mix of in-house and third-party code. How to migrate efficiently?
options: Elastic Beanstalk for PHP apps (auto-scaling, zero-downtime deployments), RDS Aurora MySQL for databases, Route 53 routing per app, S3 + CloudFront for static assets, DMS for MySQL migration, database parameter store for per-app config
Migrate one website at a time manually
explain: AWS App Mesh with Envoy sidecar proxies provides traffic policy enforcement at the service level without code changes. Retry count, timeout, and circuit breaker settings per service pair are configured in App Mesh virtual nodes. X-Ray traces flows across all 500+ services. Auto-scaling groups per SLA tier ensure dedicated capacity. Direct service-to-service calls with client-side retry (option B) creates inconsistent retry behavior across 500+ services and no centralized observability.

q: Your organization operates NoSQL database (MongoDB) on-premises with 500GB data, 10K documents/sec throughput. Needs AWS migration. Some operations require transactions (ACID). How to migrate?
options: AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) for MongoDB → DynamoDB (with transformation), or MongoDB Atlas on AWS for MongoDB-compatible API, DynamoDB global tables for multi-region, provisioned capacity tuned to 10K DPS, conditional writes for transaction simulation
Keep MongoDB on-premises, add cloud layer
explain: Redshift Concurrency Scaling enables separate compute queues for BI (OLAP) and ML workloads, preventing resource contention. Materialized views cache frequent BI aggregations. SageMaker accesses Redshift data via Redshift Spectrum or JDBC. Row-level security enforces access per user. Separate Redshift + EMR (option B) requires data duplication and increases storage costs significantly for petabyte-scale datasets.

q: Your organization runs Apache Spark analytics jobs on-premises cluster (500 nodes, 10TB working set). Jobs run nightly (6 hour batches). How to migrate to AWS cost-effectively?
options: AWS Glue (serverless Spark) replaces cluster, auto-scales per job, scales 0→500 cores elastically, S3 acts as data lake, provisioned capacity for baseline, Spot for variable capacity, saves 70% vs on-prem cluster amortization
explain: Lambda Provisioned Concurrency eliminates cold start latency, ensuring P99 <100ms. VPC Gateway Endpoints for DynamoDB remove the NAT Gateway hop, reducing latency by 10-50ms. Amazon DAX provides in-memory DynamoDB caching with sub-millisecond reads. Step Functions Express Workflows scale instantly without the 5-minute state machine overhead of Standard Workflows. Lambda in VPC with ENI allocation (option C) introduces cold start delays due to ENI attachment time.

q: Your organization has complex on-premises network (site-to-site VPN, ExpressRoute, multiple data centers). Cloud migration requires hybrid networking. How to design migration?
options: Single AWS region via single VPN

AWS Direct Connect for high-bandwidth hybrid connectivity, AWS Transit Gateway for multi-region + on-premises hub-spoke routing, AWS VPN as backup, gradual data center decommission with traffic shift via Route 53, hybrid DNS (Route 53 resolver endpoints)
explain: AWS Batch with Spot Fleet achieves 60-80% cost savings versus on-demand EC2. Job bookmarks enable incremental processing, reducing re-run costs for partial failures. S3 multipart upload/download optimizes throughput for 100TB input. On-demand EC2 (option A) would cost 5x more than Spot for the same 50B operations. Lambda with SQS (option D) has a 15-minute execution limit, preventing 5-60 minute batch jobs.

q: Your organization runs multi-tier application (web, app, database tier) on-premises. Each tier has independent scaling requirements. Cloud migration must minimize code changes. How to architect?
options: AWS Elastic Beanstalk for web tier (auto-scaling), ECS for app tier (containerized), RDS for database tier, Application Load Balancer for tier communication, auto-scaling groups per tier based on independent metrics, minimal code changes (add CloudWatch metrics emission)
Move all tiers to EC2 with same architecture
explain: Kinesis Data Streams handles high-throughput ingestion. Kinesis Data Analytics provides real-time SQL aggregations and anomaly detection. Firehose delivers to S3 for durable archival. Lambda handles complex transformations requiring custom code. The fully managed Kinesis stack achieves sub-5-second end-to-end latency. AWS Glue for batch ETL (option A) introduces multi-minute batch windows incompatible with the sub-5-second latency requirement.

q: Your organization has geographically distributed offices with local on-premises servers, each with local storage. Needs consolidation to AWS. Local latency requirements must be met. How to migrate?
options: Single AWS region, all users remote access

AWS Local Zones co-located with major offices (LA, NYC, Dallas) for <1ms latency, parent region for non-latency-sensitive workloads, AWS Direct Connect from offices to parent regions, S3 multi-region replication for data residency, Route 53 latency-based routing
explain: SageMaker distributed training with model parallelism via Horovod manages gradient synchronization across 1000+ GPUs. Elastic Fabric Adapter provides RDMA-based inter-node communication with <5 microsecond latency, critical for gradient exchange. Spot instances reduce GPU training costs 60-70%. Outposts extends SageMaker to on-premises hardware. Single EC2 p4d (option C) has at most 8 GPUs, far below the 1000+ GPU requirement.

q: Your organization has legacy vendor software with license tied to on-premises MAC address/hardware. Cloud migration breaks licensing. How to maintain compliance?
options: AWS License Manager for license tracking, vendor negotiation for license portability, MAC address spoofing via EC2 user data (if vendor allows), license affinity via placement groups to ensure same hardware allocation, AWS licensing marketplace for vendor solutions
explain: Client-side encryption with asymmetric keys ensures data is encrypted before it leaves the application, with AWS having no access to plaintext. S3 Object Lock prevents deletion. VPC endpoints for S3 prevent internet data access paths. GuardDuty detects intrusion patterns. Macie classifies PII/PHI. AWS CloudHSM-backed KMS provides FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware security. S3 with server-side encryption and IAM policies (option C) delegates key management to AWS, not meeting HIPAA BAA requirements for some organizations.

q: Your organization operates Microsoft Exchange (email/calendar) on-premises (50,000 users). Migration to O365 needed without business disruption. How to migrate?
options: AWS DataSync for hybrid connectivity setup, Microsoft Hybrid Agent enables gradual migration (batches of users per week), co-existence period allows client updates, Route 53 alias for mailbox routing, post-migration mail flow rules redirect legacy systems, mailbox verification per wave before proceeding
explain: Kinesis Data Streams ingests the transaction stream. Lambda invokes SageMaker real-time inference endpoints with provisioned concurrency for P99 <100ms. A/B testing between model versions enables canary deployment of updated models. Hourly SageMaker training jobs retrain the model on new fraud patterns. DynamoDB caches transaction history for feature engineering (recent locations, merchant history). Rule-based detection (option A) cannot adapt to novel fraud patterns and has high false positive rates.

q: Your organization runs complex build/test/release pipeline for software product (1000+ daily builds, 50+ AWS accounts for test environments). Pipeline manually orchestrated. Needs AWS-native automation. How to architect?
options: AWS CodePipeline orchestrates stages, CodeBuild for compilation+testing (cross-account roles for 50 test accounts), CodeDeploy for release, S3 for artifacts, ECR for container images, CloudFormation for test environment provisioning/teardown, per-developer cost tracking
explain: Tiered multi-tenancy provides cost efficiency: Aurora cluster per high-touch tenant (with SLA isolation guarantees) versus shared database with row-level security for standard tenants. VPCs per data residency domain satisfy geographic requirements. Cross-account isolation for premium tenants provides the strongest blast radius containment. A shared database with just a tenant_id column (option C) provides no isolation—one tenant's query can impact others and schema changes affect all tenants.

q: Your organization has Kubernetes on-premises (200+ apps, 500 pods). Migration to AWS required. Some apps need strict latency (<5ms), others batch processing. How to migrate?
options: EKS (for latency-sensitive workloads) + Fargate/EC2 mix (batch workloads), Karpenter for cost-optimized auto-scaling, cluster autoscaler for latency-sensitive nodes (dedicated capacity reservations), Helm for app deployment consistency, cost tracking via tags, no application code changes
explain: AWS Lake Formation provides centralized metadata catalog with fine-grained table-level, column-level, and row-level permissions for 500 users. S3 partitioning by date/source optimizes Athena query performance. Apache Iceberg adds ACID transactions to S3, enabling time-travel queries. QuickSight with Lake Formation RLS enforces permissions at the BI tool layer. Glue crawler + Athena (option A) lacks the column-level security and ACID transaction capabilities needed for 500 users with different access levels.

q: Your organization operates Time-Series Database (InfluxDB) for monitoring (metrics from 100K sources, 1B metrics/day). Needs cloud migration. 6-month lookback required. How to migrate efficiently?
options: Amazon Timestream for managed time-series, AWS DMS for historical data migration (bulk load + incremental replication), CloudWatch agent for metric collection, S3 for 6-month archive via Intelligent-Tiering, Grafana datasource for InfluxDB compatibility via Timestream
explain: EKS Anywhere extends Kubernetes to on-premises with the same control plane API as AWS EKS. AWS App Mesh with Envoy sidecars provides a unified service mesh across hybrid environments. X-Ray distributed tracing works across on-premises and cloud microservices. TGW enables private cross-cluster networking. Separate management per environment (option A) creates operational silos, prevents unified observability, and increases the risk of configuration drift between environments.

q: Your organization has 100+ third-party SaaS integrations (Salesforce, ServiceNow, HubSpot, etc.) in legacy system. Cloud migration requires maintaining integrations. How to architect?

Batch operations reduce invocation count (fewer invocations = lower cost). ElastiCache Redis caching reduces downstream API calls. CloudFront reduces egress costs. Minimizing Lambda memory (option D) slows execution, potentially increasing total duration cost more than the memory savings.

q: Your org runs recommendation engine: expensive ML model training (8hr per week). Inference happens 1M times/day. How to optimize cost without sacrificing quality?
options: Batch model training weekly (8hr), SageMaker inference endpoint (CPU-only cheaper), A/B testing old vs new model (canary 5% of traffic), ensemble predictions (weighted blend), cost: 70% reduction vs daily retraining
explain: Redshift Spectrum enables querying cold S3 data without loading into Redshift. Materialized views cache hot dashboard queries in Redshift. Scheduled start/stop (scale down off-hours) reduces compute costs. Moving from 40 to 20 Redshift nodes with Spectrum for cold data maintains query performance for dashboards while reducing costs 50%. Keeping 40 nodes (option B) wastes 60% of compute during off-hours.

q: Your org operates analytics platform: dashboard queries (1 sec), ad-hoc queries (1 min), background ML jobs (1 hr). Mixed workload. How to optimize warehouse cost?
options: Single Redshift cluster with all workloads
Redshift for dashboard (provisioned), Athena for ad-hoc (on-demand pay-per-query), EMR for ML jobs (Spot instances), cost: dashboard $10K, ad-hoc $2K, ML $1K vs $25K single cluster
explain: Selective replication by data temperature reduces unnecessary transfer: hot data (daily access) justifies daily replication; cold data (monthly access) uses monthly batch transfer via AWS Snowball for economies of scale. S3 Transfer Acceleration optimizes cross-region transfer for hot data. Replicating all daily (option D) wastes $120K/year on cold data that could be batch-transferred for a fraction of the cost.

q: Your org needs to run complex simulations: weather prediction, financial modeling. 10K CPU cores needed for 6 hours daily. Fault-tolerant. Budget: minimize compute cost. How to implement?
options: AWS Batch with Spot Instances (80% savings), checkpoints every 1hr (fault tolerance), auto-scaling to 10K cores as needed, S3 for simulation results, cost: $200/day vs $1000 on-demand
explain: Self-service FinOps scales to 500+ developers without adding staff: SCP-enforced tagging ensures cost visibility, dashboards provide self-service cost analysis, automated anomaly alerts go directly to teams, Lambda remediation handles common waste patterns, and the 2-person FinOps team focuses on strategic initiatives. One FinOps engineer reviewing all requests (option B) creates an unacceptable bottleneck at 500+ developer scale.

q: Your org operates blockchain-based supply chain: track shipments globally, immutable audit trail. 1M shipments/day, ledger grows 100GB/month. How to store ledger cost-effectively?
options: QLDB for transaction logging (immutable), S3 for ledger archival (Glacier after 90 days, Deep Archive after 1yr, cost ~$1K/month vs enterprise blockchain $100K+)
explain: ServiceNow → Lambda → Organizations CreateAccount API → Control Tower Account Factory → CloudFormation StackSets provision VPC, logging, and IAM baselines in parallel enables 30-minute SLA. Manual account creation by AWS admins (option C) typically takes 1-3 weeks and cannot scale to multiple concurrent requests.

q: Your org has legacy investment portfolio system (COBOL, 30 years old, mission-critical). Cloud migration risk: extremely high. How to minimize risk?
options: Strangler fig: new investment orders via cloud API, legacy handles existing portfolio (gradually depletes), parallel validation for 12 months, rollback capability maintained throughout, automated reconciliation daily
explain: GuardDuty findings in SQS provide a processing buffer. Lambda checks whether the source IP is in an approved maintenance IP list (stored in DynamoDB or Parameter Store). If approved, the Lambda whitelists the IP in the security group before closing port 22. If unapproved, port 22 is immediately closed. SNS notifies the team. Always closing port 22 (option B) would lock out legitimate maintenance access.

q: Your org runs custom CRM on-prem. Cloud migration: buy Salesforce (CRM as a Service) or build on AWS? TCO analysis needed.
options: TCO 5-year: Salesforce $1M/year + customization $200K = $6M total, AWS-based CRM $200K migration + $100K/year + 2 engineers ($200K/year) = $2.2M total. Choose AWS-based if in-house engineering < Salesforce cost. Hybrid: Salesforce + custom extensions via AWS Lambda.
explain: IAM Access Analyzer identifies which roles have active sessions. CloudTrail provides per-role usage history. Monthly manager email with per-user access report and confirmation link automates the recertification workflow. Auto-revocation after 2-week non-response enforces the process. Annual recertification (option C) is insufficient for SOC 2 and PCI-DSS requirements that mandate quarterly or monthly reviews.

q: Your org runs 24/7 workload with predictable hourly variation (peak 8am-6pm, off-peak other). Cost: $50K/month all on-demand. How to reduce via scheduling?
options: Baseline: Reserved Instances (24/7), peak hours: on-demand (8am-6pm only), auto-scaling to handle peak, cost: $20K baseline (RI) + $15K peak (on-demand) = $35K/month (30% savings)
explain: CloudTrail analysis identifies accounts with no API activity for 90 days. Day 90: tag as abandoned. Day 100: SNS alert to account owner. Day 110: automated backup of all resources to S3, then account closure via Organizations CloseAccount API. Exception process preserves legitimate archive accounts. Keeping all accounts indefinitely (option B) wastes management account limits and creates compliance audit complexity.

q: Your org operates SaaS: unit economics $5/customer/month cost, price $15/customer/month. As scale increases 10x, cost per unit increases (data transfer, compute increases). How to prevent margin erosion?
options: Cost optimization roadmap: caching (reduce compute 20%), CDN (reduce transfer 30%), database optimization (reduce storage 15%), total: cost/unit reduces to $3 (40% improvement), maintain $15 price → margin expands
explain: Config conformance packs validate VPC peering, routing table, and security group configurations. VPC Flow Logs provide connectivity analysis. Lambda generates GraphML architecture diagrams from the Config resource relationships. Security teams review diagrams + flow logs. CloudTrail logs network change history. Manual AWS Console audit (option D) cannot examine 500+ resources systematically in a quarterly timeframe.

q: Your org uses Reserved Instances (400x m5.large 1-year). 6 months in, instance family prices drop (m6i 10% cheaper). How to avoid locking in old family?
options: Flexible commitment: convert m5.large to m5.xlarge (same cost, right-size), or sell 200 RIs on RI Marketplace ($1.40/unit, recover 70%), buy m6i RIs with proceeds, net: locked-in pricing with flexibility via marketplace
explain: AWS Audit Manager automates evidence collection from Config rules, CloudTrail logs, Security Hub, and AWS APIs. It generates compliance reports per control framework (SOC 2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA). Electronic signatures from managers provide audit-acceptable attestation. 7-year evidence retention satisfies regulatory requirements. Manual spreadsheets (option B) create evidence gaps and require 100+ hours quarterly, which scales poorly.

q: Your org has cloud bill: $5M/month. 20% is waste (idle resources, over-provisioning). How to identify + eliminate waste systematically?
options: Systematic waste identification: Compute Optimizer (right-sizing), AWS Trusted Advisor (unused resources), Cost Anomaly Detection (unusual spikes), Config rules (non-compliant resources), estimated: $1M waste identified/month, quarterly cleanup cycles
explain: SCP deny conditions on AWS service APIs for non-approved regions (aws:RequestedRegion) prevent resource creation outside permitted regions at account level. Config rules detect any resources created (e.g., via SCP exceptions) outside approved regions daily. Lambda auto-remediates (delete or tag for migration) with CloudTrail audit. Training alone (option B) is not a technical control and cannot be enforced at scale across 185 accounts.

q: Your org implements chargeback to business units. IT costs $10M/year, distributed: compute $4M, storage $2M, network $2M, shared services $2M. How to allocate shared services fairly?
options: Shared services allocated per usage: compute team uses TGW (network charge), storage team uses encryption (security charge), Lambda team uses Secrets Manager (security charge), allocation: usage-driven, incentivizes optimization, enables precise cost attribution per business unit
Equal split per unit ($200K each, 50 units)
No shared service allocation (buried in overhead)
explain: Tiered budget enforcement: 80% triggers CloudWatch alarm notification, 90% requires manager approval via SNS (preventing autonomy loss), 100% blocks non-critical services (dev/test) but preserves production. Real-time QuickSight dashboard provides spend visibility. Monthly reviews adjust budgets based on business growth. Hard budget cap at 100% (option C) blocks legitimate production spending during legitimate business demand spikes.

q: Your org operates on-prem PostgreSQL: 5TB, 500 concurrent users. Cloud migration to RDS/Aurora needed. Binary compatibility 99% (99.99% SQL compatibility). How to migrate with minimal risk?
options: AWS DMS with binary replication (Logical Replication) for compatibility assurance, parallel Aurora cluster for validation (2 weeks), cross-validation queries on both systems, gradual application cutover (5% per day), rollback procedure pre-tested
explain: Network Load Balancer with TLS SNI-based routing directs protocol traffic to dedicated target groups. API Gateway handles REST (with Lambda integration for transformation) and GraphQL (via AppSync or HTTP API). gRPC target group uses HTTP/2 natively via ALB listener rule. Unified Lambda@Edge authorizer provides single API key validation. Three separate endpoints (option C) violates the unified routing requirement and fragments the API surface.

q: Your org has NetSuite ERP (cloud-based). On-prem extensions (custom code) interact with NetSuite via API. Cloud migration: keep extensions in cloud. How to minimize re-work?
options: Keep existing extensions on AWS (EC2 or Lambda), update API calls for cloud networking (VPC endpoints), deployment via CodePipeline, no business logic rewrite needed, cost: similar or lower vs on-prem hosting
explain: Kinesis Data Analytics with statistical anomaly detection models (Random Cut Forest) processes sensor data in real-time. Amazon Timestream provides time-series storage optimized for sensor data with automatic data tiering. AWS IoT Core manages device connections. Lambda implements custom alerting logic. QuickSight provides dashboards. Batch analysis nightly (option B) cannot detect anomalies within 1 second of occurrence.

q: Your org needs multi-region active-active: databases sync across 3 regions, RPO < 1 second, RTO < 5 minutes. How to architect at scale?
options: DynamoDB Global Tables (RPO < 1s guaranteed), Aurora Global Database for SQL data, Route 53 health checks trigger region failover (automatic RTO <5min), data consistency eventual (strong within region)
explain: Amazon Neptune scales to 5B+ graph edges with graph-optimized storage. ElastiCache Redis caches frequently traversed graph paths (friend recommendations, common connections). Neptune read replicas scale read-heavy workloads. OpenSearch provides full-text user search. DynamoDB with denormalization (option B) cannot efficiently traverse social graph connections without full table scans.

q: Your org runs complex financial system: trades, settlement, accounting. Data: 100TB, queries: analytical (1hr+), real-time (sub-second). How to minimize database cost for mixed workload?
options: Redshift for analytics (1hr+ queries), in-memory cache (DAX or Redis) for real-time (sub-second), S3 for archival (3+ years), cost: $15K Redshift + $5K cache vs $50K unified expensive database
explain: Kinesis Data Analytics with tumbling and sliding windows enables complex event pattern matching (same card in 2 countries within 1 hour). Lambda implements conditional logic combining Kinesis analytics output with DynamoDB state (recent card transactions and locations). DynamoDB provides sub-millisecond state lookups for card transaction history. Rule engine alone (option A) cannot detect temporal patterns across multiple events efficiently.

q: Your 200-account org needs to implement guardrails preventing any account from exceeding $10K/month cost. Overspend = account auto-quarantined. How to balance speed vs safety?
options: Cost threshold: $9K = alert, $10K = SCP blocks new creation (existing workloads continue), $11K = account audit by FinOps team, $12K = escalation to CFO, quarantine only if policy violated (not just cost)
explain: SageMaker Neo compiles models for specific edge hardware (ARM Cortex-A, x86) reducing model size by 40% and improving inference speed 2x. IoT Greengrass Core provides the edge runtime and model lifecycle management. Cloud training weekly via SageMaker updates models with new data. IoT Jobs staged rollout distributes updates safely. Kubernetes on edge (option A) requires 4-8GB RAM, far exceeding the 1GB RAM constraint.

q: A 240-account org needs to prevent shadow IT: unapproved SaaS tools, unapproved cloud services. How to enforce allowlist technically?
options: SCPs deny CreateResource for non-approved services per OU, API Gateway allowlist for APIs, proxy all internet traffic through WAF (block unknown SaaS domains), Config rules detect non-compliant resources, exception process for approval
explain: OpenSearch Snapshot API enables a zero-downtime initial backup. Logstash provides continuous replication from on-premises to OpenSearch. Dual-read validation ensures query result equivalence. Gradual traffic shift via client configuration (5% per day) limits blast radius. Snapshot + restore (option B) requires cluster downtime during reindexing, violating the zero-downtime requirement.

q: Your org needs distributed lock management for critical sections: 100K concurrent requests, mutual exclusion required, lock timeout 5 seconds. How to implement?
options: Amazon ElastiCache (Redis Cluster) for lock manager (sub-millisecond latency), Redlock algorithm (mutual exclusion across nodes), lock TTL (5 seconds auto-expiry), Lambda for critical section code, estimated: <10ms lock acquisition
explain: Strangler Fig with API Gateway as facade allows the monolith to handle legacy endpoints while new features are built as microservices. Facade API bridges legacy dependencies via synchronous calls. Async messaging (SQS/SNS) decouples new microservices from the monolith. Gradual monolith retirement (feature by feature) avoids high-risk big-bang rewrite. Complete rewrite (option B) requires understanding all 2M lines before writing a line of new code.

q: Your org has EXA database on-premises. Cloud migration: AWS does not have EXA equivalent. How to approach?
options: AWS Alternative: RDS for MySQL/PostgreSQL (if replatformable), or Redshift (if analytical), or DynamoDB (if NoSQL acceptable). Migrate via AWS Database Migration Service + custom script for EXA-specific features. Evaluate replacement based on workload type.
explain: AWS Directory Service Managed Microsoft AD supports multi-site replication via Site and Services configuration. AD Connector federates existing on-premises LDAP trusts with AWS AD. Gradual DC promotion in AWS creates a secondary AD site. Site-to-Site VPN carries replication traffic. Per-region AWS Directory Service (option B) creates fragmented forest trust relationships that don't support the 10-forest topology.

q: Your org runs multi-tenant SaaS with 1000 customers. Per-customer cost varies 10x (large customers use more resources). How to build multi-tier pricing model that customers accept?
options: Usage-based pricing: track per-customer: compute (vCPU-hours), storage (GB), data transfer (GB), build cost formula per metric, pricing = cost × 3x markup (enterprise, standard, startup tiers), transparent cost calculator for customers (build trust)
explain: AWS Transfer Family SFTP endpoint creation with a DNS CNAME alias enables transparent migration. Dual-run for 2 weeks allows existing customer connections to continue on old endpoint. Monitoring old endpoint for staleness identifies remaining customers. On-premises decommission after 2 weeks of zero traffic ensures all customers migrated. Requiring immediate customer action (option A) risks disrupting customer file transfer workflows with no fallback period.

q: Your 180-account org needs to enforce security standards automatically. Non-compliant resources auto-tagged for remediation. How to prevent false positives?
options: Compliance detection: Config rule + manual verification (2-person rule), SNS alert before remediation (team can cancel), dry-run remediation (show what would change), approval gate for breaking changes (terminate resource requires CFO approval)
explain: Selective provisioned concurrency for the 80 high-volume functions (1M/month) eliminates cold start overhead where it matters most. Right-sizing memory per function (via Lambda Power Tuning tool) finds optimal memory/duration trade-off. Lambda Layers reduce package size (smaller functions load faster). Standard concurrency for 420 low-volume functions avoids paying for unused reserved capacity. All provisioned concurrency (option C) would cost more than the current $50K/month.

q: Your org implements disaster recovery for entire data center (100+ applications, heterogeneous infrastructure, RTO 4 hours). How to test DR without breaking production?
options: AWS Application Discovery Service maps dependencies, Replication Manager fails over to secondary region monthly (automated), CloudFormation validates environment (smoke tests), production traffic unaffected (secondary environment isolated), metrics validate RTO <4 hours
explain: Phased migration aligns cloud spend growth with hardware depreciation schedule: Year 1 keeps 50% on-premises ($5M hardware + $2.5M AWS = $7.5M vs $10M budget), Year 2 moves to 25% ($6.25M), Year 3 goes fully cloud ($5M). Budget stays within the $10M constraint. Accelerating migration (option C) creates budget overrun in Year 1 when hardware and cloud costs overlap fully.

q: Your org negotiates AWS pricing: $100M annual spend, request 25% discount. AWS offers 15%. How to bridge gap?
options: Negotiation strategy: commit 3-year (earns 25%), or commit $75M annually (earns 18%), or hybrid: $60M 3-year + $25M 1-year = earn 22% blended. Result: negotiate 3-year + 1-year combo to reach target discount with flexibility.
explain: Dual metrics (cost target + quality metrics including latency, availability, feature velocity) prevent engineers from cutting legitimate features to hit cost targets. Bonus requires both metrics to be met. Shared savings incentive aligns engineering motivation with organizational goals. Cost-only bonus (option A) incentivizes feature removal, service degradation, and reduced HA that harms user experience while appearing to save money.
